name: LabVIEW CI
description: Portable, repo-agnostic CI/CD capabilities for LabVIEW repositories: mass compile, VI Analyzer, VIDiff comparison reports, VI snapshots / VI Browser, and a status dashboard. Driven entirely by this catalog so the configurator UI and the installer stay in sync and new capabilities are a one-entry change.

history.comment: Release notes for the tooling, newest first. `version` (above) is the CURRENT published release and MUST equal releases[0].version. The dashboard version badge and the What's New dialog read these notes to tell a consumer what changed between the version they run and the latest this source repo publishes. Each published version should also be tagged v<version> in this repo so consumers can pin an update to a specific release.

releases.title: Linux Beta image installs NI worker packages with apt when nipkg is absent
releases.summary: The current NI LabVIEW Linux base image has apt/dpkg but no nipkg executable. The experimental Linux Beta image now falls back to apt-get for ni-vialin-labview-support and ni-vipm, then continues to the VIPM/VIPC apply step.
highlights: Uses nipkg when present, but falls back to apt-get on the current nationalinstruments/labview:latest-linux image
Installs ni-vialin-labview-support and ni-vipm through the package manager available in the base image
Keeps repository VIPC files staged separately for the Linux Beta VIPM apply step

releases.title: VIDiff report deploy no longer races itself on GitHub Pages
releases.summary: Adds the shared report-pages-deploy concurrency group to the VIDiff deploy workflow so the Windows and Linux report deploys queue instead of pushing the gh-pages branch at the same instant, which previously made one run fail with a non-fast-forward "fetch first" rejection while a fresh install ran every check at once.
highlights: The VIDiff deploy workflow now serializes its GitHub Pages push with the mass-compile, VI Analyzer, unit-test, and Antidoc report deploys
Windows and Linux VIDiff reports completing together no longer make one deploy run fail on a rejected gh-pages push
cancel-in-progress is false so a queued VIDiff deploy still publishes its report rather than being dropped

releases.title: Linux Beta image build finds NI Package Manager outside PATH
releases.summary: Makes the experimental Linux Beta Dockerfile locate the NI Package Manager executable before installing VI Analyzer support and VIPM, and prints a package-manager inventory if the current NI base image does not include nipkg.
highlights: The Linux Beta image no longer assumes nipkg is on PATH before installing ni-vialin-labview-support and ni-vipm
If the NI Linux base image omits nipkg entirely, the build now reports the available package manager commands and likely package-manager files
Repository VIPC staging remains isolated to the Linux Beta VIPC input directory before the VIPM apply step

releases.title: Linux Beta image build computes its VIPC content tag correctly
releases.summary: Fixes the Linux Beta image workflow's worker-version calculation after the first experimental push proved the repository VIPC staging path works but stopped before Docker ran because of an indentation typo in the Python hash step.
highlights: The Linux Beta workflow now reaches the Docker build after staging repo VIPC files such as example/Dependencies.vipc
The worker hash includes only the staged Linux Beta VIPC input directory, keeping tooling VIPC files out of the test image
Stable Linux actions remain on the NI base image unless the experimental Linux Beta worker is explicitly selected

releases.title: The first container run after a fresh install no longer times out waiting for the worker image build
releases.summary: After installing into a new repository, the first container CI run (mass compile, VI Analyzer, VIDiff, snapshots, unit tests, Antidoc) could fail after five minutes with 'No Build LabVIEW CI Image run found ... then re-run this job', even though the worker image build was running the whole time. The wait step saw that the install commit changed worker inputs (the tooling Dockerfiles and VIPM assets under .github/) and then waited for a build keyed to that exact commit -- but a fresh install dispatches the build via workflow_dispatch on the branch tip, which is a different commit, and .github changes never trigger a per-commit build at all. The wait now prefers the repository-wide build whenever one is in progress or queued, so it waits for the build that is actually running and proceeds once it finishes. A real project dependency change (a project .vipc, which does trigger a build on its own commit) still waits for that per-commit build as before.
highlights: The post-install container run waits for the in-progress worker image build instead of timing out looking for a per-commit build that a fresh install never creates
The wait prefers the repository-wide Build LabVIEW CI Image run when one is in progress or queued (covers install/Configure Workers dispatches on the branch tip)
A project .vipc dependency change still waits for the build triggered on its own commit; steady-state runs still proceed immediately

releases.title: Experimental Linux Beta worker applies VIPM/VIPC dependencies
releases.summary: Adds an opt-in Linux Beta worker image that installs VIPM, applies repository VIPC files during the image build, publishes a workers/linux-beta manifest, and can be selected by Linux container activities without changing the stable Linux base-image path. Experimental capabilities are now first-class catalog entries and stay out of Apply to New Repo defaults unless the Experimental features card is selected.
highlights: Adds Build LabVIEW CI Image - Linux Beta, a separate GHCR image lane that installs ni-vialin-labview-support and ni-vipm, applies configured VIPC files, and publishes linux-beta worker manifests
Lets Linux Mass Compile, VI Analyzer, and VIDiff keep using the NI base image for empty/base/none while routing latest or Linux Beta tags to the linux-beta worker image
Keeps Antidoc, Linux Beta, Linux VIPM/VIPC support, and Windows VI Browser 2.0 snapshots behind a deselected Experimental features card in Apply to New Repo

releases.title: A job started while the worker image is rebuilding reliably waits, even through a brief Actions-API hiccup
releases.summary: When a worker container image is being (re)built and someone pushes a change or enqueues a job in the meantime, that job's container activities (mass compile, VI Analyzer, VIDiff, snapshots, unit tests, Antidoc) already wait for the build to finish before they run, on every Windows and Linux container workflow. This release closes a small gap in that guarantee: the check that decides whether a build is in progress made a single Actions-API call, and if that one call failed transiently (a blip or a momentary rate limit) the job would treat it as 'nothing building' and start early on a stale or half-built image. The check now retries a failed API call a few times before deciding, so a transient hiccup no longer lets a job slip past the wait. A genuine permissions gap still falls through without waiting, so CI is never wedged.
highlights: Jobs enqueued while the worker image is rebuilding wait for the build to finish, then run on the freshly built image -- on all Windows and Linux container workflows
The in-progress-build check now retries on a failed Actions-API call instead of assuming 'nothing is building' and starting early
A real actions:read permission gap still never wedges CI (the check falls through after a few quick retries)

releases.title: Windows container jobs wait for the Docker engine instead of failing when a hosted runner starts up without it
releases.summary: GitHub's hosted windows-2022 runner pool is currently, intermittently, starting jobs before the Docker engine is up -- or on a VM where the docker service has not been registered yet (actions/runner-images#14252 and #13888). When a job landed on one of those runners, the worker image build and the Windows container activities (mass compile, VI Analyzer, VIDiff, snapshots, unit tests, Antidoc) failed immediately with 'failed to connect to the docker API ... the system cannot find the file specified' or 'Docker service not found on this runner', even though re-running usually lands on a healthy runner. Every Windows job that uses Docker now runs a shared ensure-docker step that waits up to three minutes for the engine to respond -- starting, or if necessary registering, the docker service first -- and only fails, with a clear message, if Docker never comes up. The worker image build and the VIDiff Windows job, which previously had no such guard at all, are now covered too. Healthy runners are unaffected (the check returns in about a second).
highlights: A shared ensure-docker.ps1 waits for and starts the Docker engine, so a hosted-runner Docker hiccup no longer turns into an immediate red build
The worker image build and the VIDiff Windows container job, which had no Docker readiness check before, now use it too
Clear, actionable error if Docker never starts (re-run to get a healthy runner, or use a self-hosted Windows runner) instead of a cryptic npipe failure

releases.title: Container CI works on repos whose default branch is not main, and the worker image build no longer fails on a missing Dockerfile
releases.summary: Two fixes make a fresh container install actually run on more repositories. First, the worker image build was failing immediately with 'the system cannot find the file specified' because the install copied only labview-ci.Dockerfile, not the two Dockerfiles the Windows build actually uses (labview-vipm-base.Dockerfile and labview-vipc-layer.Dockerfile, introduced when the worker was split into a shared base plus a VIPC layer). The install now vendors all three, so Build LabVIEW CI Image succeeds and the container activities (mass compile, VI Analyzer, VIDiff, snapshots, unit tests, Antidoc) get a real image. Second, the container workflows assumed the default branch was named main: the 'Restore CI scripts' step ran 'git fetch origin main' and failed with exit 128 ('couldn't find remote ref main') on a repo whose default branch is master, and the push trigger only fired on main. The fetch now uses the repository's actual default branch, and Apply to New Repo rewrites the push-trigger branch to the target repo's default branch at install time, so push-to-default CI fires regardless of the branch name.
highlights: Worker image build no longer fails on a missing Dockerfile: the install now vendors labview-vipm-base.Dockerfile and labview-vipc-layer.Dockerfile alongside labview-ci.Dockerfile
Container workflows fetch CI scripts from the repository's default branch instead of a hardcoded 'main', fixing the exit-128 'couldn't find remote ref main' failure on master-default repos
Apply to New Repo rewrites the workflow push-trigger branch to the target repo's default branch, so push-to-default CI runs on repos that don't use 'main'

releases.title: Re-running Apply to New Repo now repairs a non-markdown README too
releases.summary: When a repository already had the dashboard section added to a non-markdown README (e.g. ReadMe.txt, where GitHub shows the markdown as raw text instead of rendering the badge and link), re-running Apply to New Repo now creates a proper README.md so the dashboard link renders. Previously the re-install detected the existing marker and skipped the README entirely, leaving the badge unrendered. The original plain-text file is left untouched, and the previously injected non-rendering section is dropped from the new README.md so it is not duplicated. Markdown READMEs that already link the dashboard are still left alone.
highlights: Re-installing into a repo whose README is plain text (ReadMe.txt, etc.) now migrates the dashboard badge into a real README.md that actually renders, instead of skipping it
The earlier non-rendering section spliced into the plain-text README is removed from the generated README.md so the section is not duplicated
Markdown READMEs already linking the dashboard remain untouched (no duplicate sections)

releases.title: Fresh installs build the worker container image automatically and CI waits for it
releases.summary: Installing into a new repository now makes the container CI work end to end without a manual step. When the custom-image capability is installed, Apply to New Repo dispatches the Build LabVIEW CI Image workflow the moment the install merges, so the worker container image (ghcr.io/<owner>/<repo>-labview, built with your baked dependency VIPC) exists for the first run. Container activities (mass compile, VI Analyzer, VIDiff, snapshots, unit tests, Antidoc) now wait for an in-progress image build instead of failing with a confusing manifest unknown / Failed to start container error. The README dashboard badge is also added correctly when the repository has a non-markdown README (e.g. ReadMe.txt): a proper README.md is created so the badge and link render instead of showing raw markdown.
highlights: Apply to New Repo builds the worker container image right after the install merges, so container CI is ready without a separate manual build
Container CI activities wait for an in-progress worker-image build (with clear guidance on timeout) instead of failing on a missing image
Non-markdown READMEs (ReadMe.txt, etc.) get a real README.md with the dashboard badge so the link renders, leaving the original file untouched

releases.title: Dashboard dialogs no longer vanish mid-install when the page auto-refreshes
releases.summary: The dashboard auto-refresh that keeps the page live while CI runs no longer reloads the page while a dialog is open, so Apply to New Repo (and Configure, VI Analyzer, Unit Testing, and What's New) can finish without the dialog disappearing on its own. Previously a busy dashboard reloaded about once a minute via an HTML meta refresh and a queued-run fast reload, which destroyed the open install iframe before it could show the result -- even though the install itself had completed.
highlights: Auto-refresh is now a guarded JavaScript timer instead of an uncancellable HTML meta refresh; it reloads on the same cadence but waits while a dialog is open and reloads once it is closed
The queued-run fast reload defers the same way, so neither refresh path can blow away an in-progress install
Apply to New Repo now reliably shows its Done summary with the pull request and dashboard links instead of the dialog closing unexpectedly

releases.title: VI Browser 2.0: paging through cases no longer zooms the diagram
releases.summary: In the position-aware VI Browser (Snapshot 2.0) renderer, clicking through a Case/Event/Sequence structure's cases no longer randomly zooms the block diagram. Double-clicking a case selector arrow (or the Fit button) is now ignored by the zoom handler, so the diagram size stays fixed while you navigate cases.
highlights: Double-clicking the case selector arrows or the Fit button no longer triggers the double-click-to-zoom action.
Background double-click-to-zoom on the diagram itself is unchanged.
Case navigation and the diagram fit/size are unaffected otherwise.

releases.title: Dependencies page surfaces the Linux note as a column info popover
releases.summary: The standalone "Linux VIPC support is coming" banner on the Dependencies page is gone. The Linux column header now carries the same info (i) marker the Win Beta column uses, and clicking it opens a popover explaining that the Linux container does not yet apply repository VIPC configurations, so Linux package coverage shows as missing for now. The Win Beta popover, which previously rendered far too narrow near the right edge of the table, now opens at a stable readable width.
highlights: Replaces the page-wide Linux VIPC banner with a compact info popover on the Linux column header, matching the Win Beta treatment.
Gives the column info popover a fixed 320px width so it no longer collapses to one word per line near the right edge.
Keeps the same explanation text; only its presentation moved from a banner to an on-demand popover.

releases.title: Dependencies table uses aligned checkbox cells
releases.summary: The Dependencies page now keeps each runner-platform column fixed and centered so package rows line up with their headers. Dependency presence is shown as a checked box only; the table no longer labels cells with yes, exp, or other visible qualifiers.
highlights: Fixes runner-platform column alignment across VIPM/VIPC, NI Packages, and System Components tables.
Shows installed/present dependencies with a checked checkbox instead of yes or exp text.
Leaves missing, pending, unsupported, and not-used cells visually blank so the table scans by presence only.

releases.title: Dashboard mobile cards align correctly
releases.summary: The CI Dashboard mobile layout now keeps each activity card row aligned as label-left and result-right, even when result cells carry inline desktop alignment. The mobile context controls are also tightened so the dashboard uses less vertical space before the revision cards.
highlights: Moves the mobile dashboard card rules late in the stylesheet so they win over generic table and cell styles.
Forces activity labels to stay left-aligned and result chips/buttons to stay right-aligned on phone-width cards.
Tightens the mobile dashboard filter and Columns controls in the shared header context bar.

releases.title: Dependencies page: Win Beta column with a click-to-explain note
releases.summary: The experimental Windows container column on the Dependencies page is now labelled Win Beta, and its column header is clickable: clicking it reveals a short note explaining that Win Beta is a separate, isolated Windows container where new capabilities and new action runners are built and proven -- such as getting VI Browser 2.0 snapshots to render on Windows -- so the in-progress work never disrupts the containers production actions rely on, until it is ready to replace the main Windows container.
highlights: Renames the experimental Windows column to Win Beta across the VIPM/VIPC, NI Packages, and System Components tabs.
Makes the Win Beta header clickable to reveal an explanatory popover (closes on a second click, an outside click, or Escape), replacing the always-on banner.
No behaviour change to the existing Windows and Linux columns or to the opt-in EXPERIMENTAL_WINDOWS_IMAGE render lane.

releases.title: Transparent worker-container rebuilds with in-flight coordination
releases.summary: Rebuilding the worker container is a lengthy (15+ minute) step, so it is now coordinated and transparent. Before a rebuild starts, LabVIEW CI handles CI that is already running against the container according to a configurable policy -- drain (let running jobs finish first, the default) or cancel (stop them now) -- and only ever touches jobs that started before the rebuild, so there is no wait deadlock with the tasks that already wait for the new image. The FAQ now explains exactly when a rebuild happens, how long it takes, and how in-flight work is handled.
highlights: Adds a coordinate step to Build LabVIEW CI Image that drains or cancels in-flight worker-container CI before the rebuild, per container.rebuildPolicy in .github/labview-ci.yml (default drain) or a rebuild_policy dispatch input.
Skips jobs newer than the rebuild (already gated by wait-for-worker-image) so draining never deadlocks, and is best-effort so it never blocks the actual rebuild.
Documents the worker-container rebuild lifecycle -- triggers, the 15+ minute duration, the queue-and-wait behavior, and drain vs cancel -- in the FAQ.

releases.title: Experimental Windows container lane on the Dependencies page
releases.summary: The Dependencies tables gain a third Windows (experimental) column so an isolated experimental Windows container can be tracked alongside the production Windows worker and Linux. Production actions keep running on the standard Windows worker; the VI Browser 2.0 Windows render opts into the experimental image only when the repository variable EXPERIMENTAL_WINDOWS_IMAGE is set, so new container work can be proven before it replaces the main Windows container. Until that image is published the experimental column is shown as experimental / pending.
highlights: Adds a Windows (experimental) column to the VIPM/VIPC, NI Packages, and System Components tabs.

Wires an opt-in EXPERIMENTAL_WINDOWS_IMAGE repository variable into the Windows VI Browser 2.0 render so experimental runs use an isolated container and skip the production worker-build wait; unset leaves behaviour unchanged.
Shows the experimental column as experimental / pending until its image manifest is published, without affecting the existing Windows and Linux columns.

releases.title: Dependencies source tables move into tabs
releases.summary: The Dependencies page now keeps VIPM/VIPC packages, NI Package Manager installed dependencies, and System Components in one shared table area with tabs. This keeps the dependency-source breakdown from the previous release while making the page easier to scan vertically.
highlights: Adds tabs for VIPM / VIPC, NI Packages, and System Components on the Dependencies page.
Shows only the selected dependency-source table at a time, so the page no longer stacks all three tables vertically.
Keeps the same generated dependency inventory and Linux VIPC pending behavior.

releases.title: Dashboard builds time out stalled GitHub API reads
releases.summary: The dashboard generator now gives GitHub API reads a bounded timeout, matching the existing timeout used for Pages JSON reads. This prevents a stalled API request from leaving the dashboard publish step running indefinitely while preserving the dependency-source table update from the previous release.
highlights: Adds a 20-second timeout to dashboard GitHub API requests.
Lets a fresh dashboard publish cancel and replace a stuck dashboard-pages run through the existing workflow concurrency group.
Keeps the new VIPM, NI Package Manager installed dependencies, and System Components tables intact.

releases.title: Dependencies page publishes separate dependency-source tables
releases.summary: Publishes the dependency-source table update with the dashboard and catalog in sync. The page now separates VIPM/VIPC packages, NI Package Manager installed dependencies such as LabVIEW and NI Unit Test Framework, and repo-provided system components such as the VI Browser 2.0 toimages Go renderer.
highlights: Renames the NI section to NI Package Manager Installed Dependencies to make its source explicit.
Keeps the generated dashboard dependency inventory and published catalog on the same release.
Preserves the VIPM/VIPC table and Linux VIPC pending state while showing NIPM and system-component context separately.

releases.title: Dependencies page separates VIPM, NI packages, and system components
releases.summary: The Dependencies page now groups dependency coverage by source instead of putting everything into a single VIPC table. VIPM/VIPC packages remain in their own table, NI Package Manager-installed dependencies such as LabVIEW, VI Analyzer support, Unit Test Framework, and VIPM are listed separately from worker manifests, and repository-provided system components such as the VI Browser 2.0 toimages Go renderer are called out in a third table.
highlights: Adds a dedicated NI Package Manager Dependencies table that surfaces LabVIEW and NI Unit Test Framework alongside other NI-installed tooling from worker manifests.
Adds a System Components table for CI runtime pieces that do not come from VIPM or NIPM, including the VI Browser 2.0 toimages Go renderer.
Keeps the existing VIPM/VIPC package table and Linux VIPC pending banner intact while still showing Linux NI package and system-component context.

releases.title: Windows runners share the repo worker by default
releases.summary: Windows LabVIEW action runners now default to the repository's shared worker image instead of mixing base-image and worker-image defaults by action. Mass Compile, VIDiff, VI Snapshots, and Windows VI Browser 2.0 join VIA, Unit Tests, and Antidoc on the shared Windows dependency container unless a repo explicitly opts an action back to base. The Dependencies page now collapses package coverage to Windows and Linux columns, with Linux still marked pending until Linux dependency application is implemented.
highlights: Makes Mass Compile, VIDiff, VI Snapshots, and Windows VI Browser 2.0 default to ghcr.io/<owner>/<repo>-labview:latest.
Keeps config.container.use and per-action config.container.actions overrides, including base, as explicit escape hatches.
Collapses the Dependencies table to Windows and Linux OS-level coverage columns.

releases.title: Windows VI Browser 2.0 zero-frame runs fail visibly
releases.summary: Windows VI Browser 2.0 render runs now propagate the Go runner process exit code reliably from the PowerShell container entrypoint. If the Go runner fails or a non-empty worklist produces zero frame JSON files, the workflow fails instead of finishing green with no Windows 2.0 output.
highlights: Refreshes the runner process before reading ExitCode so failed Go render batches return non-zero from the container entrypoint.
Treats a missing runner exit code as a failed render instead of allowing PowerShell to exit 0.
Adds a final non-empty worklist / zero-output guard so Windows 2.0 runs cannot report success without published frames.

releases.title: Dependencies page flags Linux VIPC support as pending
releases.summary: The Dependencies page now clearly explains that Linux containers do not yet apply repository VIPC configurations automatically. Linux package coverage is intentionally shown as missing until Linux VIPC application is implemented, while Windows worker coverage continues to use the configured worker manifests.
highlights: Adds a prominent Dependencies page banner explaining that Linux VIPC configuration support is coming.
Forces Linux dependency columns to show VIPC packages as missing instead of implying repo VIPCs were baked into the Linux container.
Keeps generated dependency inventories honest by marking Linux VIPC coverage unsupported for now.

releases.title: Windows VI Browser 2.0 uses the Go render path directly
releases.summary: The Windows VI Browser 2.0 container no longer blocks rendering on a separate PowerShell COM-readiness probe. It now prepares the LabVIEW container and immediately hands off to the same Go batch runner used by the Linux 2.0 path, letting lvctl own LabVIEW attach, launch, readiness, and per-VI rendering. The shared runner now fails when a non-empty batch produces zero frames, the Windows entrypoint times out a stuck Go runner while printing its captured logs, and newer manual Windows render runs replace stuck superseded runs.
highlights: Removes the PowerShell GetActiveObject gate that prevented the Go renderer from running in the Windows container.
Keeps the existing Go runner/lvctl toolchain as the owner of LabVIEW startup and rendering on Windows.
Fails a non-empty Go render batch when every VI fails, while still allowing partial successes to publish.
Adds a Windows runner timeout that terminates stuck LabVIEW/lvctl runs and prints runner stdout/stderr before exiting.
Lets newer Windows 2.0 render dispatches cancel older in-progress runs in the same workflow.

Leaves workflow failure propagation in place so real Windows render failures remain visible.

releases.title: Windows VI Browser 2.0 render failures are reported correctly
releases.summary: Windows VI Browser 2.0 runs now fail the workflow when the LabVIEW Windows container cannot start the COM-backed renderer, instead of reporting success with zero published frames. The renderer also waits longer for LabVIEW to become COM-ready in the stock Windows container so slow first launches have a chance to render before the run is marked failed.
highlights: Propagates non-zero Windows render exit codes out of the workflow so failed LabVIEW startup is visible in the VI Browser.
Extends the COM-ready wait for the in-container LabVIEW startup from four minutes to ten minutes.
Keeps zero-frame renderer failures from being mistaken for successful runs that simply skipped a VI.

releases.title: Mass Compile dashboard clears completed runs
releases.summary: Mass Compile cells now prefer the deployed summary report over a still-visible pending status, so a completed manual run immediately renders its percentage result instead of a stale running badge.
highlights: Fixed a dashboard row state where a Mass Compile action could be done and successful in GitHub Actions while the dashboard still showed the compile spinner.
When masscompile/<sha>/summary.json exists, the dashboard now renders the report chip first; pending status remains only for runs whose report has not landed yet.

releases.title: Populate history can re-run every selected activity
releases.summary: The Populate dashboard history dialog now lets Re-run all include selected activity rows that only have missing results. Re-run now queues every selected non-snapshot revision for the activity, replacing existing results where present and filling gaps where no prior result exists.
highlights: Makes Re-run all select VI Analyzer, Antidoc, and other rows even when the selected revisions only have gaps for that activity.
Keeps snapshots as Render-only because the snapshot backfill workflow is content-addressed and only renders missing blobs.
Updates the Re-run tooltip so the dialog explains that existing results are replaced while missing ones are filled.

releases.title: Dashboard document switching opens the selected revision directly
releases.summary: Removes the redundant Activity selector from VI Browser and report contexts now that Dashboard owns document switching. Dashboard report links now pass the report viewer a first-party relative source path, so changing from one document type to another opens the current revision immediately instead of landing on an empty viewer until the revision is reselected.
highlights: Removes the old context-bar Activity selector so Dashboard is the single document-switching menu.
Report links now use the current revision when present, or the newest known revision otherwise.
Report viewer links pass relative iframe sources, so Mass Compile, VI Analyzer, Unit Tests, and Antidoc open content immediately.

releases.title: Linux VI Browser 2.0 renders fall back from stale image overrides
releases.summary: Fixes manual Linux VI Browser 2.0 snapshot runs that appeared successful while rendering nothing because the TOIMAGES_IMAGE variable pointed at an old private image. The workflow now tries the requested override first, falls back to the repository-owned render image when the override cannot be pulled, and fails a manual run loudly if no render image is available at all.
highlights: A stale or unauthorized TOIMAGES_IMAGE value no longer blocks Linux 2.0 rendering when the repo-owned image exists.
The renderer passes the actually pulled image into run-toimages.sh, so fallback runs render and publish frames normally.
Manual runs now fail visibly if no render image is pullable instead of ending green without producing frames.

releases.title: Dependencies selector shows revision container config
releases.summary: The Dependencies page revision dropdown now stays active when a generated dependency inventory is available. It lists repository revisions, switches the table to the selected revision, and shows that revision's config.container routing so users can see which VIPC files and action-specific container tags were configured at that point in history.
highlights: Keeps the revision dropdown enabled instead of locking the page to the dashboard build inventory.
Loads each selected revision's .github/labview-ci.yml and displays its container.use, container.vipc, and per-action routing.
Caches repository revision metadata in dependencies/index.json so the selector still has revisions when the live commits API is unavailable.

releases.title: Dashboard activity links use the current or latest revision
releases.summary: The top-level Dashboard navigation now groups Overview, VI Browser, Mass Compile, VI Analyzer, Unit Tests, and Antidoc together. Revision-specific entries resolve the same way across the header: they use the revision already selected or shown by the current page, and fall back to the latest known VI snapshot revision when no current revision is available. The report revision picker also uses the same report availability fallback as Activity, checking index.html when summary metadata is missing.
highlights: Dashboard is now a dropdown that exposes the revision-specific dashboard activities in one place.
VI Browser and report links use the current selected revision when one is available, otherwise the latest known revision.
The report revision picker now shares the Activity fallback from summary.json to index.html.

releases.title: Activity switcher opens reports when the page exists
releases.summary: The VI Browser Activity switcher now treats a report as available when either its summary.json or its index.html exists for the selected revision. Previously it only trusted summary.json, so choosing Mass Compile, VI Analyzer, Unit Tests, or Antidoc could appear to do nothing even when the report page itself had been generated. The switcher still avoids missing reports, but now falls back to the real report page before showing the 'none yet' message.
highlights: Activity report probes now fall back from summary.json to index.html for the selected revision.
Existing report pages open from the VI Browser Activity switcher even if their summary metadata is missing.
Missing reports still stay guarded, so the switcher does not intentionally navigate to a 404.

releases.title: Windows VI Browser 2.0 dispatches render the selected revision
releases.summary: Running a Windows VI Browser 2.0 render from the browser no longer dispatches the workflow at a raw commit SHA, which GitHub can reject with HTTP 422. The page now dispatches the workflow on the repository's default branch and passes the selected revision as a target_sha input. The Windows workflow then materializes that target revision before building the worklist and rendering, so the generated <blob>.windows.json frames match the VI Browser revision the user selected.
highlights: Fixes the HTTP 422 error when clicking Run the Windows 2.0 snapshot from the VI Browser.
Passes the selected revision as target_sha instead of using it as the workflow_dispatch ref.
Renders from a checked-out copy of target_sha so Windows 2.0 frames match the selected revision.

releases.title: VI Browser 2.0 renders can be generated from the Windows container
releases.summary: The Windows VI Browser 2.0 path now uses the same cross-platform Go toimages renderer as the Linux path, built as Windows binaries and run inside the stock NI LabVIEW Windows container. Windows renders are published to Pages as <blob>.windows.json with a json-blobs.windows.json index, so the VI Browser's Windows tab can show the same in-place presentation as Linux once frames are generated. The Windows workflow skips only existing Windows JSON, so Linux <blob>.json files no longer hide missing Windows renders, and the catalog now installs the workflow plus its shared renderer assets.
highlights: Adds the Windows VI Browser 2.0 workflow and shared toimages renderer assets to the snapshots capability, so consumers receive the Windows render path when they update.
Publishes <blob>.windows.json files and json-blobs.windows.json to Pages beside existing snapshot HTML and Linux <blob>.json frames.
Fixes Windows worklist seeding so existing Linux 2.0 frames never mask missing Windows 2.0 frames.

releases.title: Header menus are unified into Settings and Tools
releases.summary: The desktop header now uses one consistent secondary menu model across dashboard, settings, report, and informational pages: Settings holds configuration sections, and Tools holds page-specific actions plus Clients, About, What's New / update status, and Appearance. The old Help plus three-dot split is removed, so the menus no longer duplicate each other or stack into overlapping popovers.
highlights: Replaces the Help + three-dot split with a single Tools menu for shared utilities and page-specific actions.
Keeps Settings as the only configuration menu and makes the same Settings / Tools structure available across pages.
Opening Settings or Tools closes the other menu, eliminating the visible overlap state.

releases.title: Dependency-aware worker image gating and dashboard inventory
releases.summary: Repo VIPC dependency changes now block downstream LabVIEW pipeline tasks until the matching worker image build has finished and the rebuilt GHCR image is available. The shared header shows a repo-wide banner while the worker image is updating, and the dashboard adds a top-level Dependencies page that lists every VIPC file, the packages inside it, and which runner/container columns currently include those packages.
highlights: Adds a shared worker-image wait helper used by LabVIEW container workflows so VIPC changes do not run against stale containers.
Shows a global dashboard banner while Build LabVIEW CI Image is queued or running, explaining that pipeline tasks are waiting for the rebuilt GHCR image.
Adds the Dependencies tab with a VIPC/package tree and per-runner coverage columns backed by the generated dashboard dependency index.

releases.title: Dashboard and VI Browser fit mobile screens
releases.summary: The dashboard revision table now switches to stacked cards at mobile and narrow-tablet widths, with filter controls and result badges wrapping inside the viewport instead of forcing horizontal overflow. The VI Browser now stacks its file tree above the viewer on phone-sized screens, so the sidebar no longer consumes nearly the whole width or pushes the viewer off the right edge.
highlights: Dashboard revision rows collapse into width-safe cards earlier and wrap their controls cleanly on small screens.
VI Browser uses a stacked mobile layout with a bounded file tree and a full-width viewer below it.
A mobile audit at 390px and 320px confirmed the dashboard, VI Browser, and the rest of the dashboard pages avoid page-level horizontal overflow.

releases.title: Help and the three-dot menu are consolidated
releases.summary: The top navigation now keeps shared utilities in one Help menu: Clients, About, What's New / update status, and Appearance. The three-dot menu is no longer a second utility menu; it only appears on pages that have extra context-specific commands, such as Populate history or VI Browser renders on the dashboard. This keeps settings and informational pages cleaner while preserving every existing action.
highlights: Help now owns Clients, About, What's New / update status, and Appearance in one predictable place.
The three-dot menu hides on pages with no extra context actions instead of duplicating the Help menu.
Dashboard-specific commands still live in the three-dot menu, so Populate history and VI Browser renders remain available.

releases.title: Settings pages share a sub-navigation, so the configuration sections feel like one area
releases.summary: The per-repo configuration pages -- Configure Workers, VI Analyzer, and Unit Testing -- now show a Settings sub-navigation strip in the context bar directly under the header, with the section you are on marked. Previously each was an isolated page you could only reach by reopening the Settings menu; now they read as one Settings area you can move through in a click, matching how the Revision and Activity bars work on the other surfaces. The Settings item in the top navigation already highlights while you are on any of these pages.
highlights: Configure Workers, VI Analyzer, and Unit Testing now share a Settings sub-navigation with the current section marked.
The sub-navigation sits in the same context bar used for the Revision / Activity controls elsewhere, for a consistent layout.
Shared header asset only -- every configuration page gets it automatically, on the source site and consumer dashboards.

releases.title: In-pane reports gain a Back-to-VIs link, a one-click full report, and a loading state
releases.summary: Polishing the unified Explorer: when a report (Mass Compile, VI Analyzer, Unit Tests, Antidoc) is framed in the VI Browser pane, a small bar above it now offers a Back to VIs link (one click back to the project tree), an Open full report link that opens the standalone report viewer -- where Regenerate / Re-run and the raw report live -- and a brief loading indicator while the report loads. The framed report also carries an accessible, report-specific title, and the bar wraps gracefully on narrow screens.
highlights: Back to VIs returns from a framed report to the project tree in one click.
Open full report opens the report viewer with its Regenerate / Re-run and raw-report controls.
Added a loading indicator and an accessible per-report title for the framed report.

releases.title: Activity switcher: grouped lenses and a deep-link that opens on the right one
releases.summary: The Activity switcher (in the VI Browser and report context bars) now groups its lenses -- Code & changes, Quality, and Artifacts -- so the list stays legible as more activities are added, instead of one long flat menu. Opening a shared VI Browser link that points at a report (a lens parameter in the URL) now also starts the Activity dropdown on that report, so the menu matches the framed view from the first paint.
highlights: Activity lenses are grouped (Code & changes / Quality / Artifacts) for a switcher that scales.
A report deep link now selects the matching entry in the Activity dropdown on load.

releases.title: VI Browser: open a revision's reports right in the pane (unified Activity switcher)
releases.summary: Picking Mass Compile, VI Analyzer, Unit Tests or Antidoc from the Activity switcher while you are in the VI Browser now frames that report directly in the browser pane, instead of navigating away to a separate report page. The shared Revision and Activity pickers stay put at the top, so you can flip between a revision's VIs and its reports without losing your place; the project tree is hidden for these per-revision reports (they are not per-VI). The view is shareable -- the address bar carries a lens parameter, and opening such a link restores the framed report. This is the first step of the unified Explorer that merges the VI Browser and the report viewer into one surface.
highlights: Reports (Mass Compile, VI Analyzer, Unit Tests, Antidoc) now open in place inside the VI Browser pane.
The Revision + Activity pickers stay in the shared context bar; switch between VIs and reports without a page change.
Report views are deep-linkable (a lens parameter in the URL) and restore on load; picking a report a revision lacks shows a brief note instead of switching.

releases.title: Restore the Activity switcher's Snapshots entry and the in-VI-Browser picker (regressed by a stale-base change)
releases.summary: A recent change to the shared header was built on an older copy of the file and unintentionally reverted two shipped capabilities of the unified Activity switcher: the 'Snapshots' (VI Browser) entry in the report Activity picker (3.25.0), and the Activity picker that mounts inside the VI Browser so you can jump from a revision's VIs straight to any of its reports (3.26.0). This release restores both, so the Activity switcher is once again bidirectional and spans a revision's full activity set -- snapshots plus the four reports. No other behavior changes.
highlights: The report Activity picker again offers 'Snapshots' (VI Browser) as the first entry.
The VI Browser again shows the Activity picker beside its Revision selector, with live-sha availability checks at click time.
Pure restoration of 3.25.0 + 3.26.0 picker code that a stale-base edit had reverted -- no other changes.

releases.title: Documentation is now a top-level nav link, and overlapping top-bar menus are fixed
releases.summary: The developer Documentation page is now promoted to a first-class entry in the top navigation bar (between VI Browser and Settings) instead of being tucked inside the Help dropdown, so it is easy to find. On a consumer dashboard it opens the source site's documentation.html in a new tab, matching the existing About-link convention (same-tab on the source site). Also fixed a minor UX bug where two top-bar menus (Settings, Help, Share, and More) could be open and visually overlap at the same time - opening any one of them now closes the others, so only a single menu is ever open.
highlights: Documentation moved from the Help dropdown to a top-level nav link.
Documentation opens in a new tab on consumer dashboards (same-tab on the source site).
Only one top-bar menu (Settings / Help / Share / More) can be open at a time - no more overlap.

releases.title: Bake portable Git into the worker so VIPM 26.3 can verify the public repo (UTF / -350053)
releases.summary: The 3.26.1 public-repo workaround still failed in the container with a second flavour of exit 6: 'error: Cannot determine repository visibility: failed to execute git: program not found'. VIPM 26.3 Community Edition does not just read .git/config - it shells out to a real git binary to confirm the repository is public, and the Windows base image ships no git. (The earlier 'no git binary needed' finding held only because the local test machine had git on PATH.) The Dockerfile now bakes in portable MinGit (the official git-for-windows redistributable, GIT_INSTALLER_URL) at C:\git and prepends it to PATH, and install-vipc.ps1 ensures git is discoverable before invoking vipm. With a git binary present, VIPM reads the fabricated .git origin, confirms this public repo over the network, and the .vipc tooling (Antidoc, Caraya, VI Tester, and the UTF JUnit Report library) finally bakes in - so headless RunUnitTests stops failing with LabVIEW CLI error -350053.
highlights: Fixes the second exit-6 failure ('Cannot determine repository visibility: ... git: program not found') by baking portable MinGit into the worker image so VIPM can verify the public repo.
VIPM 26.3 Community Edition shells out to a real git binary to check repository visibility - it is not enough to fabricate .git/config; a git executable must be on PATH.
Adds a GIT_INSTALLER_URL build-arg (default: the official MinGit 64-bit zip) and documents the git-binary requirement in .github/labview/vipm/README.md.

releases.title: Worker bakes .vipc dependencies under VIPM 26.3 Community Edition (fixes 'requires a public Git repository', UTF / -350053)
releases.summary: With the VIPM 26.3 CLI flags fixed, the worker's headless 'vipm install' got past argument parsing but then failed instantly (exit 6) with 'VIPM Community Edition requires a public Git repository' - so the .vipc tooling (Antidoc, Caraya, VI Tester, and the UTF JUnit Report library) was still not baked in and headless RunUnitTests kept failing with LabVIEW CLI error -350053. VIPM 26.3 Community Edition only installs when the current working directory is inside a PUBLIC Git repository; the image build runs from C:\vipm, which is not a Git repo. We verified against the real 26.3 CLI that VIPM only reads .git/config's origin URL (and checks the repo is public) - it needs no git binary, no clone, and no commits. install-vipc.ps1 now fabricates a minimal .git whose origin points at this public repo (passed as the VIPM_PUBLIC_REPO_URL build-arg, defaulting to this repo; the build workflow supplies the actual building repo so forks use their own), runs the installs from there, and removes it afterward. The .github/labview/vipm/README.md documents the requirement and the minimal .git layout.
highlights: Fixes the exit-6 'VIPM Community Edition requires a public Git repository' failure so the worker's .vipc dependencies (including the UTF JUnit Report library) are baked in and headless RunUnitTests stops failing with -350053.
install-vipc.ps1 runs 'vipm install' from a fabricated minimal .git (HEAD + origin pointing at the public repo) - no git binary, clone, or commits needed - verified against the real VIPM 26.3 CLI.
Adds a VIPM_PUBLIC_REPO_URL build-arg (the build workflow passes the building repo's URL) and documents the Community Edition public-repo requirement in .github/labview/vipm/README.md.

releases.title: Open any of a revision's reports from inside the VI Browser
releases.summary: The Activity picker now also appears in the VI Browser, beside the revision selector. While browsing a revision's VIs you can switch straight to its Mass Compile, VI Analyzer, Unit Tests, or Antidoc report -- and from a report you can come back to the VI Browser -- so one consistent switcher moves you between all of a revision's activities from either side. Because the VI Browser changes revision in place, the picker always targets the revision you are currently viewing, and quietly tells you when a report has not been produced for it instead of opening an empty page.
highlights: The Activity picker now appears in the VI Browser too, so you can open any of a revision's reports while browsing its VIs.
One switcher works in both directions -- report to VI Browser and VI Browser to report -- always for the revision you are viewing.
If a report was not produced for the current revision, the picker says so rather than opening an empty page.

releases.title: Jump from a report straight to the VI Browser for the same revision
releases.summary: The Activity picker on a report now lists Snapshots alongside the other checks, so from any report -- Mass Compile, VI Analyzer, Unit Tests, Antidoc -- you can open the VI Browser for the SAME revision in one step, without going back to the dashboard. The picker now spans a revision's whole activity set (its VI snapshots plus every report), the first half of one unified switcher that will also appear inside the VI Browser in a later step. Reports that were not produced for the revision stay greyed out; Snapshots is always available.
highlights: The report Activity picker now includes Snapshots, opening the VI Browser for the current revision in one step.
It now covers a revision's full activity set -- VI snapshots plus every report -- not just the reports.
Reports missing for the revision stay disabled; Snapshots is always offered.

releases.title: Worker dependency installs match the VIPM 26.3 CLI (fixes 'unexpected argument --refresh', UTF / -350053)
releases.summary: After the worker was upgraded to VIPM 2026 Q3 (26.3.3954), headless 'vipm install' failed instantly (exit 2) with "error: unexpected argument '--refresh' found" - so no .vipc dependencies were baked in and headless UTF still failed with LabVIEW CLI error -350053. The 26.3 CLI changed shape from the older 2026.1.0 build: it removed the '--refresh' option from 'install' and replaced it with a separate 'vipm refresh' command, and '--labview-version' / '--labview-bitness' are now global options that go before the subcommand. install-vipc.ps1 now runs 'vipm refresh' once and installs with 'vipm --labview-version <YYYY> --labview-bitness <32|64> install <packages>', with a fallback to the bare form (which targets the active LabVIEW from the seeded Settings.ini) if a CLI build rejects the flag position. Verified against the real 26.3 CLI: the command parses and returns success. The .github/labview/vipm/README.md was updated to document the 26.3 command shape and its differences from 2026.1.0.
highlights: Fixes the exit-2 'unexpected argument --refresh' failure that blocked every headless 'vipm install' on the VIPM 26.3 worker, so a project's .vipc dependencies (including the UTF JUnit Report library) are baked in again.

install-vipc.ps1 now matches the 26.3 CLI: a standalone 'vipm refresh' plus 'vipm --labview-version --labview-bitness install <packages>', with a bare-form fallback that relies on the seeded Settings.ini active target.
Updates .github/labview/vipm/README.md to document the 26.3 command shape (standalone refresh, global LabVIEW flags, '--refresh' removed) and how it differs from the older 2026.1.0 CLI.

releases.title: Worker upgrades to VIPM 2026 Q3 so headless dependency installs complete (fixes UTF / -350053)
releases.summary: With LabVIEW running headless and a seeded Settings.ini, the worker's VIPM installs got further but still aborted during the image build: each 'vipm install' hung and then failed with 'Operation VIPM command library_list timed out after 330s'. The VIPM that ships on the NI package feed (2026.1.0) cannot complete a headless package install in a Windows container - it times out querying LabVIEW and does not surface the underlying error. The worker now installs the official VIPM 2026 Q3 release (26.3.3954) directly from JKI's CDN (silent /exenoui /qn), which surfaces install errors and improves headless/container support. install-vipc.ps1 also sets VIPM_TIMEOUT so the short build-time default timeouts (VIPM only applies its longer CI timeouts when it detects GITHUB_ACTIONS, which is absent during 'docker build') no longer abort a cold, first-run headless LabVIEW. A new README under .github/labview/vipm documents the full set of requirements for baking a project's .vipc dependencies into the worker reliably.
highlights: Upgrades the worker's VIPM to the 2026 Q3 release (26.3.3954) from JKI's CDN, replacing the older NI-feed 'ni-vipm' (2026.1.0) whose CLI timed out on 'library_list' during headless container installs.
Sets VIPM_TIMEOUT so the short default operation timeouts used during 'docker build' (no CI env detected) no longer abort the install before a cold headless LabVIEW responds.

Adds .github/labview/vipm/README.md documenting how to bake a project's .vipc dependencies into the Windows worker, plus every VIPM-in-container requirement we found (Community Edition, Settings.ini, headless LabVIEW, timeouts, CLI flags).

releases.title: VI Browser drops its redundant Back to dashboard link
releases.summary: The VI Browser sidebar had its own back-link to the dashboard at the top, duplicating the shared top bar's Dashboard link and the LabVIEW CI logo that both already return you to the dashboard. It has been removed -- matching the configuration pages, which dropped the same redundant link in 3.23.1 -- so the sidebar now leads with the VI Browser title and the file tree gets a little more room. Nothing is lost: use the Dashboard link or the logo in the top bar to go back.
highlights: Removed the duplicate back-link from the VI Browser sidebar; the shared top bar's Dashboard link and logo already return you to the dashboard.
The sidebar now leads with the VI Browser title, giving the file tree slightly more vertical room.
Presentation cleanup only -- no change to how the VI Browser works.

releases.title: Worker seeds VIPM Settings.ini so installs work in a fresh image (fixes UTF / -350053)
releases.summary: The real reason the worker's VIPM installs kept aborting with 'IO error: Failed to load' was a missing file: the VIPM CLI reads C:\ProgramData\JKI\VIPM\Settings.ini for its target LabVIEW configuration and errors out (os error 2) when that file does not exist. In a freshly built image VIPM is installed from a package but never launched interactively, so Settings.ini is never created. install-vipc.ps1 now seeds a minimal Settings.ini - pointing the CLI at the image's LabVIEW (version, path, VI Server port 3363) in Community Edition - before installing, but only when the file is absent so a real VIPM install is never clobbered. With this in place the tooling install (Antidoc, Caraya, VI Tester, and the UTF JUnit Report library) completes, the JUnit library is baked into the image, and headless RunUnitTests stops failing with LabVIEW CLI error -350053. This was validated locally against the same VIPM 2026.1.0 CLI.
highlights: Fixes the persistent 'IO error: Failed to load ...Settings.ini (os error 2)': the worker now seeds a minimal VIPM Settings.ini targeting the image's LabVIEW before installing packages.
The UTF JUnit Report library is finally installed, so headless RunUnitTests emits its JUnit report and stops failing with -350053 - real unit-test results are reported.
Safe and license-free: the file is only created when absent (never clobbers a real VIPM), and VIPM runs in Community Edition with no Pro activation.

releases.title: Worker VIPM install uses the correct CLI flags (fixes UTF / -350053)
releases.summary: The previous worker fix got LabVIEW running headless for VIPM, but the install still failed because the install command used flags this VIPM CLI does not accept: it called a standalone 'refresh' command (the CLI has none) and passed '-y' (not a valid option), so every package install aborted with exit 2 and the UTF JUnit Report library was still missing - headless RunUnitTests kept failing with LabVIEW CLI error -350053. install-vipc.ps1 now uses the CLI's actual interface (verified against vipm 2026.1.0): the package list is refreshed via the global '--refresh' option on 'install' rather than a separate command, '-y' is dropped because the CLI is non-interactive by default, and it still targets the image's LabVIEW with --labview-version 2026 --labview-bitness 64. The headless-LabVIEW launch and Community Edition (no VIPM Pro license) behaviour from the previous release are unchanged.
highlights: Installs now succeed: the worker uses 'vipm install --refresh --labview-version 2026 --labview-bitness 64 <packages>', dropping the invalid 'refresh' command and '-y' flag that made every install exit 2.
The UTF JUnit Report library is finally baked into the image, so headless RunUnitTests no longer fails with -350053 and real unit-test results are reported.
Still license-free: LabVIEW is launched headless and VIPM runs in Community Edition - no VIPM Pro activation required.

releases.title: Worker installs VIPM tooling headlessly with no license (fixes UTF / -350053)
releases.summary: Installing the worker's VIPM tooling (Antidoc, Caraya, VI Tester, and the UTF JUnit Report library) by package name still failed inside a freshly built image: the VIPM CLI aborted every command with 'IO error: Failed to load', so the UTF JUnit Report library was never baked in and headless RunUnitTests kept failing with LabVIEW CLI error -350053. The root cause was that the modern VIPM CLI needs LabVIEW already RUNNING before it can install packages, but the Docker build step had no LabVIEW running - install-vipc.ps1 now launches LabVIEW headless and waits for its VI Server before installing. The CLI also runs in Community Edition (VIPM_COMMUNITY_EDITION), so installs need no VIPM Pro license or activation. (Follow-up 3.24.2 corrects the install command's flags.)
highlights: Launches LabVIEW headless before installing VIPM packages, fixing the 'IO error: Failed to load' that aborted every install in a freshly built image.
No VIPM Pro license required: installs run in VIPM Community Edition (VIPM_COMMUNITY_EDITION).
Targets the image's LabVIEW with --labview-version / --labview-bitness; install flags themselves are corrected in 3.24.2.

releases.title: Switch between a revision's reports without leaving the page
releases.summary: When you open a CI report for a revision -- Mass Compile, VI Analyzer, Unit Tests, or Antidoc -- the context bar now has an Activity picker beside the revision picker. It jumps straight to another report for the SAME revision (for example from Mass Compile to VI Analyzer) without going back to the dashboard to hunt for the right cell. Report types that were not produced for the revision are shown greyed out, so the picker never lands on a missing report. This is the first step toward a single unified explorer, where the revision, the activity, and (later) the specific VI are all chosen from one consistent place.
highlights: A new Activity picker in the report context bar switches between a revision's reports (Mass Compile / VI Analyzer / Unit Tests / Antidoc) in place.
Report types that do not exist for the current revision are shown disabled, so the picker never lands on a missing report.
It matches the revision picker and sits beside it, extending the same consistent context bar to switching activities.

releases.title: Configuration pages drop the redundant Back to dashboard link
releases.summary: The configuration pages already show the shared top bar -- with its Dashboard link and the LabVIEW CI logo that both return you to the dashboard -- so the extra "Back to dashboard" link inside each page's own title was redundant. It has been removed from Configure Workers, VI Analyzer, Apply to New Repo, and What's New, leaving a cleaner page header that matches the rest of the tool. Nothing is lost: use the Dashboard link or the logo in the top bar to go back.
highlights: Removed the duplicate "Back to dashboard" link from the Configure Workers, VI Analyzer, Apply to New Repo, and What's New pages.
The shared top bar's Dashboard link and logo already return you to the dashboard, so the page header is now cleaner with nothing lost.
Presentation cleanup only -- no change to what any page does.

releases.title: Settings and Help menus now appear on the configuration pages too
releases.summary: The configuration pages -- Configure Workers, VI Analyzer, Unit Testing, Apply to New Repo, What's New, and About -- now carry the same top-bar Settings and Help menus as the dashboard, instead of dropping back to a bare header with only a Dashboard / VI Browser link. You can now jump straight between the configuration pages from any of them, and the menu for the section you are on highlights, so it is always clear where you are in the tool. This is a navigation-consistency change only: the contents and behavior of each page are unchanged.
highlights: Configure Workers, VI Analyzer, and Unit Testing are reachable from the Settings menu on every page, not just the dashboard.
The Settings (or Help) menu highlights as the active section while you are on one of its pages, so you always know where you are.
Navigation only -- no change to what any configuration page does.

releases.title: Dashboard search and filters move into the sticky toolbar
releases.summary: The dashboard's search box, the status filter (All / Failed / Running / Passed), the Include CI-only revisions toggle, and the Columns customizer have moved out of the page body and into the shared toolbar that sits directly under the header -- the same context bar the report and VI Browser pages already use for their revision selector. Because that bar is sticky, the controls now stay in view while you scroll a long list of revisions, so you can refine the search or switch the status filter without scrolling back to the top of the page. It also makes the whole tool feel consistent: every page now keeps its primary controls in the same place. This is a layout change only -- the search, filters, and column menu behave exactly as they did before.
highlights: Search, the status filter, the CI-only toggle, and Columns now live in the sticky toolbar under the header instead of in the page body.
The controls stay visible while you scroll the revision table, so you can filter without jumping back to the top.
Consistent placement: the dashboard now shares the same context bar the report and VI Browser pages use.

releases.title: Two new FAQ entries: where worker containers live, and what an Actions runner is
releases.summary: The FAQ now answers two common infrastructure questions. 'Where are the worker containers stored and where do they run?' explains that your worker image is stored in the GitHub Container Registry (GHCR) as a package under your own repository (ghcr.io/<owner>/<repo>-labview), built with the built-in GITHUB_TOKEN, and that it runs on the GitHub Actions runner that executes each job - a fresh container is pulled, started with your repo mounted, and destroyed when the job ends, all inside your own GitHub Actions environment. 'What is an Actions runner?' explains that a runner is the machine that executes a workflow job, and the difference between GitHub-hosted runners (disposable windows-2022 / ubuntu-latest VMs) and self-hosted runners you provide.
highlights: New FAQ entry 'Where are the worker containers stored and where do they run?' - images live in GHCR as packages under your repo; containers run and are torn down on the GitHub Actions runner, never on NI's or the author's servers.

New FAQ entry 'What is an Actions runner?' - explains GitHub-hosted vs self-hosted runners, where the worker container actually executes, and how the max concurrent runners cap relates to GitHub's per-account concurrency limit.
Documentation only - no change to any workflow, container, or dashboard behaviour.

releases.title: CI runs now show which revision they check and who requested them
releases.summary: Every container CI run -- VI Analyzer, Mass Compile, VIDiff, Unit Tests, and Antidoc, on Windows and Linux -- now gets a descriptive title in the Actions list instead of an anonymous entry that looks identical to every other run. The title shows the revision the run is checking, who requested it, and how it was triggered, so a run you started from the dashboard Run / Re-run button is easy to find and tell apart. When you launch a check from the dashboard the title carries the exact commit you picked (not just the branch tip the workflow technically ran against), making it simple to confirm a run is doing what you expected. This is a presentation-only change: it adds no new workflow inputs, so it is safe for already-installed repositories and changes nothing about how runs are dispatched.
highlights: Each CI run is titled with the revision it checks, the requester, and the trigger (dashboard or manual dispatch vs push / pull request).
Dashboard-launched runs show the exact commit you selected, not just the branch tip the workflow ran against.
Presentation-only -- no new workflow inputs, so existing installs keep dispatching exactly as before.

releases.title: The VI Browser revision bar gains previous / next steppers
releases.summary: The VI Browser's revision picker -- now in the shared context bar -- gets the same previous / next steppers as the report pages. Click the left chevron to step to the newer revision or the right chevron to step to the older one, and walk through a VI's history one revision at a time without opening the dropdown. The steppers disable themselves at the newest and oldest revisions, so you always know where you are in history. The revision control now behaves identically whether you are reading a report or browsing VIs.
highlights: The VI Browser revision bar adds previous / next steppers, matching the report context bar.
Step to the newer or older revision with one click; the steppers disable at the ends of history.
The revision control now behaves the same in the VI Browser and on the report pages.

releases.title: Worker now installs VIPM tooling by package name (fixes headless UTF / -350053)
releases.summary: The worker image build applied its VIPM tooling (Antidoc, Caraya, VI Tester, and the UTF JUnit Report library) by handing the generated .vipc file to the modern VIPM CLI - but that CLI rejects the generated config.xml-only .vipc ('Code 42: this file does not appear to be a valid VI package configuration') and no longer has the legacy apply_vipc verb, so none of the tooling was actually installed. In particular the UTF JUnit Report library (ni_lib_utf_junit_report) was missing, which made the headless RunUnitTests operation fail with LabVIEW CLI error -350053. install-vipc.ps1 now reads the package list out of each .vipc's config.xml and installs each package by name using the documented 'vipm install <name>@<version>' form, which needs no VIPM Pro activation. Verified locally against VIPM 2026 Free Edition: the JUnit library installs and RunUnitTests produces its JUnit report.
highlights: Fixes headless NI Unit Test Framework runs: the UTF JUnit Report library is now actually baked into the worker, so RunUnitTests no longer fails with -350053.
Root cause: the modern VIPM CLI rejects the generated config.xml-only .vipc (Code 42) and dropped the legacy apply_vipc verb, so the old 'vipm install <file.vipc>' path was a silent no-op.
install-vipc.ps1 now installs each package by name ('vipm install <name>@<version>'), which also restores Antidoc, Caraya and VI Tester; no VIPM Pro activation required.

releases.title: The VI Browser puts its revision picker in the shared context bar, too
releases.summary: The VI Browser's revision selector now lives in the same context bar under the header as the per-revision reports, instead of being tucked into the VI Browser's own sidebar. You pick a revision in the one consistent place whether you are reading a report or browsing VIs, so the control no longer jumps to a different spot when you switch between page types. This continues the move toward one consistent revision bar across the whole tool. The selector keeps all of its revisions and behaves exactly as before; only its location changed.
highlights: The VI Browser revision picker moves out of the sidebar and into the shared context bar under the header.
Reports and the VI Browser now show the revision control in the same place, so it no longer moves when you switch views.
Display-only: the selector keeps every revision and all of its behavior; this is a location change for consistency.

releases.title: Reports get a persistent revision bar with previous / next steppers
releases.summary: On a per-revision report (Mass Compile, VI Analyzer, Unit Tests, Antidoc) the revision selector now lives in a dedicated context bar directly under the header instead of being crammed into the header actions. The bar adds previous / next steppers so you can walk to the newer or older revision of the same report with one click, and it sits in the same place for every report type. This is the first step toward one consistent revision bar across the whole tool. Display-only; the selector still opens the same report for the chosen revision.
highlights: The report revision selector moves into a persistent context bar under the header, in one consistent place.
New previous / next steppers walk to the newer or older revision of the same report in one click (disabled at the ends).
The header is less cramped, and the revision bar is the foundation for a consistent revision context across all pages.

releases.title: Grouped Settings and Help menus in the header navigation
releases.summary: The header navigation now groups the configuration and help pages into two clear top-nav menus: a Settings menu (Configure Workers, VI Analyzer, Unit Testing) and a Help menu (About, and Clients on the source site). They were previously buried together in the More overflow menu, so the configuration pages are now easier to find and the top bar reads as Dashboard / VI Browser / Settings / Help, with the green Apply to New Repo button kept prominent. The More menu still holds the remaining items (Populate history, version, appearance). Display-only; every page and link behaves exactly as before.
highlights: Configuration pages (Configure Workers, VI Analyzer, Unit Testing) are grouped under a new Settings menu in the top navigation.
About and Clients move into a Help menu, so the header reads Dashboard / VI Browser / Settings / Help.
The prominent green Apply to New Repo button is unchanged, as are all destinations; only their grouping in the header changed.

releases.title: The header activity indicator now shows running, queued and failed counts
releases.summary: The header's live CI indicator is now a compact activity pill with separate running, queued and failed counts instead of a single N-running chip. Each segment appears only when its count is above zero, the whole pill links to the repository Actions list, and it pairs with the attention banner so you can see at a glance how much CI is in flight and whether anything needs attention. Display-only; the underlying activity poll is unchanged.
highlights: The running chip becomes a run / queue / fail activity pill, surfacing queued and failed counts that were not shown before.
Each count segment shows only when it is non-zero, and the pill hides entirely when there is no activity.
It reads from the same 60-second activity poll (no extra API calls) and links to the repository Actions list.

releases.title: A failed activity now surfaces in a banner across every page
releases.summary: The shared header now shows a dismissible attention banner whenever a CI activity is currently failing: it names the workflow and the commit it failed on, and links straight to the failing run so you can jump to the error from anywhere - the dashboard, the VI Browser, or any report. The banner reflects each workflow's newest run, so a workflow that has since gone green clears itself, and dismissing a failure remembers it until a new one occurs. Nothing else about the header changes.
highlights: A red attention banner under the header names any currently-failing activity (workflow + commit) and links straight to the run.
It appears site-wide from the shared header, so an error is one click away from the dashboard, the VI Browser, or any report.
It tracks each workflow’s latest run (a fixed workflow clears itself) and a dismissed failure stays dismissed until a new one happens.

releases.title: The VI Browser 2.0 Linux / Windows switch is always visible
releases.summary: The Linux / Windows switch in the VI Browser 2.0 view now shows whenever a 2.0 render is on screen, instead of only appearing once a Windows render existed. When a VI has no Windows render yet, the Windows option is shown but greyed with a tooltip explaining it is not published for that VI / repository yet - the same convention as the greyed-out Diff option - so the control is discoverable and Linux stays the active view. It enables itself automatically once the Windows render pipeline publishes its frames.
highlights: The Linux / Windows toggle is now always visible in the 2.0 view, so you can see the platform choice exists.
Windows is greyed with an explanatory tooltip until a Windows render is published for that VI, then it enables automatically.
Linux remains the default and active render; nothing else about the 2.0 view changes.

releases.title: Switch the VI Browser 2.0 view between the Linux and Windows render
releases.summary: The in-place VI Browser 2.0 viewer can now show the render produced on Windows as well as the one produced on Linux. When a VI has a Windows render, a Linux / Windows switch appears in the 2.0 toolbar next to Front Panel / Block Diagram; picking one loads that platform's position-aware frames, and the choice is carried in the shareable URL. The switch only appears for a VI that actually has a Windows render, so the view stays exactly as before until the Windows render pipeline publishes its frames - Linux remains the default.
highlights: A Linux / Windows toggle in the 2.0 view loads the position-aware render from whichever container produced it.
The toggle appears only for VIs that have a Windows render (driven by the published render index), so there is never a dead button, and Linux stays the default.
The selected platform is reflected in the shareable / printable VI Browser URL (plat2=windows).

releases.title: The FAQ now explains how the Clients page finds and tracks repositories
releases.summary: In the spirit of full transparency, a new FAQ entry documents exactly how the Clients page is built. Discovery is pull-based: the source repository finds its own clients by querying GitHub's public search index for the labview-ci topic and for references back to this repository - your repository is never modified, never has to phone home, and no shared secret or telemetry is involved. Only public repositories are ever discovered or listed; private repositories are never included. The Clients page now links straight to this explanation.
highlights: New FAQ entry 'How do you discover and track which repositories use this tooling?' details the three public signals (the labview-ci topic, a catalog source.repo reference, and a workflow uses: reference) and how each candidate is verified.
Documents the privacy guarantees: discovery only covers public repositories, private repositories are never listed, and only information GitHub already exposes publicly is shown.
The Clients page links directly to the new FAQ answer, which opens automatically when followed.

releases.title: The dashboard revision table now collapses to cards on narrow screens
releases.summary: On phones and narrow windows the revision table no longer forces a horizontal scroll: each revision becomes a self-contained card, and every activity result (Mass Compile, VI Analyzer, VIDiff, Snapshots, Unit Tests, Antidoc) is shown on its own labelled line so you can tell what each status belongs to without a header row. On wider screens it stays the familiar table. Display-only - the data, links, and run / queue / re-run behaviour are unchanged.
highlights: Below ~820px the revision table reflows into one card per revision, removing the need to scroll sideways.
Each activity cell carries its column name as an inline label, so a result still reads clearly once the header row is hidden.
Wider screens are unchanged - the same table, rich Revision cell, search, status filter, and Columns menu all keep working.

releases.title: Returning to the dashboard shows a run you just started elsewhere, right away
releases.summary: When you kick off a run from another page - like Re-run analysis from a VI Analyzer (or other) report, or a run started in another browser tab - and switch back to the dashboard, it now reflects that queued or running run immediately. The dashboard previously only re-checked on its auto-refresh timer (up to 15 minutes when idle), so a run you had just started could look like nothing was happening until the page happened to reload. The dashboard now re-applies its optimistic queued overlays and re-syncs against GitHub the moment its tab is shown again or regains focus, so a freshly started run surfaces as soon as you return to it.
highlights: Switching back to the dashboard after starting a run elsewhere (e.g. Re-run analysis from a report) now shows it as Queued/Running right away, instead of waiting up to 15 minutes for the next auto-refresh.
The dashboard re-applies its queued overlays and re-checks GitHub on tab focus and visibility change, not only on a full page load (throttled so it never hammers the API).
No change to how runs are queued or reconciled - this only makes an already-started run surface sooner when you come back to the dashboard tab.

releases.title: Dashboard revisions render as one rich row instead of four columns
releases.summary: The dashboard's separate Commit, Message, Author, and Date columns are now a single compact "Revision" cell: a coloured author initial, the commit message, and a secondary line with the short SHA, author, and date. The same links still open the revision in the VI Browser, and the search box, status filter, run / queue / re-run overlays, and the per-activity Columns menu all keep working - the Columns menu now lists just the activity columns, since the revision identity is always shown. Display-only: no change to the underlying data or actions.
highlights: Commit / Message / Author / Date are merged into one professional Revision cell with an author avatar.
The short SHA, author, and date move to a compact secondary line; the message stays primary and links to the VI Browser.
