Existing repositories follow the pointer automatically - update checks, the Update now upgrade, and both the web and command-line installers redirect to the new home and then re-pin the repository to it.
Fixed the Windows PowerShell installer default source so a no-argument install fetches the tooling from the correct repository.

releases.title: Installing no longer floods a new repo with CI runs, and Linux container jobs are opt-in
releases.summary: Two fixes to what happens right after Apply to New Repo. First, opening the install pull request used to start the entire CI matrix - Mass Compile, VI Analyzer and VIDiff, on both Windows and Linux - even though you had not changed any of your own VIs. That happened because the tooling ships a few helper VIs (the PrintToSingleFileHtml diff helpers) and the VI workflows watched every '**.vi' path, so the install's own files tripped them. Those workflows now ignore changes under '.github/**', so a tooling-only commit or the install pull request no longer triggers them - only real changes to your VIs do. Second, the Linux container jobs run on the NI base image and are still experimental, yet they were installed and run by default and reliably failed on a fresh repo; installing now defaults to Windows only, with Linux offered as an opt-in (the installer and Configure Workers both leave Linux unchecked and label it experimental). Already-installed repositories are unchanged until you re-run the installer or reconfigure.
highlights: Opening the install pull request no longer fans out the whole CI matrix: the VI workflows now ignore '.github/**', so only changes to your own VIs trigger Mass Compile, VI Analyzer and VIDiff.

Linux container jobs (experimental, NI base image) are no longer installed or run by default - they are an opt-in in the installer and Configure Workers, so a fresh repo no longer shows failing Linux checks.
Windows is the supported default; existing installs are unchanged until you re-run the installer or reconfigure.

releases.title: Apply to New Repo: the dashboard link appears only once the page is really live
releases.summary: When the configurator auto-merges the install, it now waits for GitHub Pages to actually publish your dashboard before showing the link, so the link is never a dead 404. The install progress bar stays in an active "publishing..." state the whole time GitHub Pages is building the page (with a running timer), and only completes and reveals the clickable dashboard link once the page is confirmed live. Until then the dashboard URL is shown as plain text rather than a link you might click too early. No change to an already-installed pipeline.
highlights: Publishing the dashboard is now part of the install progress - the bar keeps showing it is working until GitHub Pages serves the page.
The dashboard link is revealed only after the page is confirmed live, so it is never a 404.
Honest status while publishing, with an elapsed-time indicator; the link appears the moment the dashboard goes live.

releases.title: Apply to New Repo: VI Browser 2.0 render setup removed from install
releases.summary: The Apply to New Repo / Integrate page no longer includes the optional "VI Browser 2.0 - position-aware renders" panel for setting a private render image and read-token. That configuration does not need to be done at install time and is still available later from the dashboard's own VI Browser 2.0 setup wizard, which provisions the same render image and token when you choose to enable the in-place 2.0 view. Because that panel was the only part of the installer that writes a repository secret, the pre-filled install token no longer requests the Secrets permission; Variables (used to pin your settings) and the other permissions are unchanged. There is no change to an already-installed pipeline.
highlights: The installer's optional VI Browser 2.0 render-image/token panel is removed - that setup is not needed at install time.
VI Browser 2.0 can still be provisioned later from the dashboard's own VI Browser setup wizard.
The pre-filled install token no longer requests Secrets (it was only used by that panel); Variables and the other permissions are unchanged.

releases.title: VI Browser 2.0 reflects real render status instead of the token prompt
releases.summary: Once a repository is provisioned, the 2.0 view no longer shows the 'needs a render token' prompt (or a 1.0 fallback) for a VI that has no position-aware frames. It now reflects the actual state: the token prompt only when the repo is not connected; the in-place render when frames exist; a 'rendering...' status with a link to the run while the snapshot action is in progress; and, when a run finished without frames for a VI, a plain explanation - controls (.ctl) and VIs without a block diagram are skipped, and a render the image could not open is skipped - plus View run and Re-run buttons. It reads the latest vi-snapshots-json.yml run via the Actions API using the dispatch token, and polls while a run is in progress.
highlights: The 2.0 view now reflects real state: token prompt / in-place render / rendering-in-progress / finished-with-no-frames or failed.
A provisioned repo no longer shows 'needs a render token' for a VI that simply has no frames - it explains why and offers View run + Re-run.
Reads the latest vi-snapshots-json.yml run status via the Actions API (dispatch token) and auto-refreshes while a run is in progress.

releases.title: Apply to New Repo: auto-tag your repository so it appears in the client directory
releases.summary: Installing via Apply to New Repo now tags your repository with the labview-ci topic so the source repository's client directory can discover and link it reliably (topic search is complete where code search is not). This uses the install token's Administration permission, which the pre-filled token now requests. If your existing token lacks it, the install still completes and a note prompts you to re-create the token with Administration so the repo can be tagged next time - tagging is best-effort and never blocks the install.
highlights: Installs tag the repo with the labview-ci topic so it is reliably listed in the source client directory.
The pre-filled install token now requests Administration (Read and write); if it is missing the install still succeeds and prompts you to re-create the token with it.
Tagging is best-effort and never blocks the install.

releases.title: VI Browser 2.0: clearer setup-token guidance (repository access + which permission)
releases.summary: The VI Browser 2.0 setup-token prompt now spells out the step the token URL cannot pre-fill: under Repository access, choose Only select repositories and pick this repo. A token left at the read-only default cannot write and returns a 403, which was the most common remaining cause of a stuck connect even after the permission params were fixed. When a token is then rejected, the error names exactly which permission is missing - 'cannot write Secrets' (check repository access plus Secrets: Read and write) versus 'the render token saved, but cannot write Variables' - so you know precisely what to fix instead of a generic message. The link still pre-checks Secrets and Variables.
highlights: Setup-token prompt now tells you to select this repository under Repository access (the step the URL cannot pre-fill, and the most common 403 cause).
403 errors now name the exact missing permission - Secrets vs Variables - and note when the secret already saved.
VI Browser 2.0 only; the token link still pre-checks Secrets + Variables (Read and write).

releases.title: Clarification: code compatibility comes from your project save setting
releases.summary: The LabVIEW version used by CI stays fixed at the year the tooling is built for, and that is unchanged. An earlier note (3.4.3) said a future update would add a separate code-compatibility selector to the configurator; that is no longer planned. Keeping your code compatible with an older LabVIEW run-time is already handled by your project's own save-for-previous-version setting stored in the .lvproj, which the container honors when it opens the project to build and analyze it - so no extra control in the configurator is needed. This release only corrects the configurator's in-code notes and this guidance; there is no behavior change.
highlights: Documentation-only clarification: the fixed LabVIEW version used by CI is unchanged.
Code compatibility with older run-times comes from your project's .lvproj save setting, which the container honors - not from a configurator option.
Supersedes the 3.4.3 note that mentioned a future code-compatibility picker; that control is not planned.

releases.title: Setup-token links now pre-select the Variables permission
releases.summary: The pre-filled fine-grained token-creation links - the VI Browser 2.0 setup token and the installer token on the Integrate page - used the wrong URL parameter for the Variables permission (variables instead of GitHub's actual actions_variables), so GitHub silently ignored it and the generated token was missing Variables: Read and write. That left the token unable to save the TOIMAGES_IMAGE render-image variable even when Secrets was granted. The links now send actions_variables, so opening the token page pre-selects both Secrets and Variables (Read and write). Secrets and Actions were already correct.
highlights: Token-creation links now correctly pre-select the Variables permission (GitHub's parameter is actions_variables, not variables).
Fixes both the VI Browser 2.0 setup token and the installer token on the Integrate page.
The pre-filled GitHub token form now has Secrets + Variables (Read and write) ready - no manual permission hunting.

releases.title: Installing the pipeline no longer auto-runs CI on a new repo
releases.summary: Deploying LabVIEW CI to a new repository used to kick off CI and image-build jobs immediately: VI Snapshots, Unit Tests, and the LabVIEW container image build auto-ran on the tooling files the install adds (their scripts, the .github/labview-ci.yml config, the Dockerfile and the tooling vipc). Now those workflows trigger only on real LabVIEW source changes - your VIs, or your own project dependency vipc - or on demand. So installing simply stands up the dashboard showing your full revision history with no runs, and the dashboard offers a one-click Run all history prompt to backfill CI across history whenever you choose. Heavy work (snapshots, tests, image builds) starts only when you change source or ask for it.
highlights: Installing no longer auto-runs CI: VI Snapshots, Unit Tests, and the LabVIEW image build now trigger only on real LabVIEW source changes (or on demand), not on the tooling files the install adds.
A fresh dashboard stands up with your full revision history and no runs, plus a one-click Run all history prompt so you choose when to backfill CI across history.
The LabVIEW image still rebuilds when your project depends on a new package (a *.vipc outside the tooling dir), monthly for base-image security, or on demand - just not on install.

releases.title: VI Browser 2.0: a rejected render-token setup token can be fixed in place
releases.summary: After the connect-hang fix, a setup token that lacked permission - for example reusing the read:packages render token, which cannot write secrets - showed a clear error but left the bad token saved, so clicking Connect again just repeated the same error with no way to enter a different one. Now a rejected or insufficient setup token (401/403/404) is dropped automatically and the prompt reopens in place with specific guidance - including that the setup token is a separate, fine-grained token with Secrets and Variables set to Read and write, distinct from the render token - so you can paste a corrected token and finish without reloading. The render token you already pasted is kept, and the same in-place recovery now applies to the Actions: write token used by the Generate the 2.0 snapshot button.
highlights: A rejected or under-scoped setup token is now cleared and re-requested in place instead of dead-ending on the error.
The prompt spells out that the setup token is a separate fine-grained token (Secrets + Variables: Read and write), not the read:packages render token.
The same in-place token recovery applies to the Actions: write token used to run the snapshot.

releases.title: Apply to New Repo publishes the dashboard first, even if a CI check fails
releases.summary: Installing the pipeline into a new repository now makes the CI dashboard and its README link the first thing that goes live - independent of any CI run. Previously the dashboard only appeared after the first analysis run, so a failing check (for example the Windows VI Analyzer) could leave the site stuck on the publishing placeholder. The configurator now squash-merges the install pull request by default and dispatches the dashboard build immediately, so the dashboard publishes within a minute of applying, and the placeholder and on-screen guidance no longer say it waits for a CI run. Changing a VI still fills in the analysis columns (VI Analyzer, VIDiff, Mass Compile), but a failing check never keeps the dashboard from publishing. Uncheck the new default to review the install pull request first - the dashboard then publishes the moment you merge.
highlights: The dashboard and README link publish as the first thing on Apply to New Repo - no CI run required, and a failing VI Analyzer no longer blocks the dashboard.
The install pull request now auto-merges by default and the dashboard build is dispatched immediately, so the site goes live within a minute (uncheck to review first).
Corrected the publishing placeholder and the installer guidance that wrongly said the dashboard appears only after the first CI run.

releases.title: LabVIEW version is fixed to the supported year
releases.summary: The LabVIEW version field on the install and Configure Workers screens is now read-only and fixed to the year the CI tooling is built for (2026). The worker container images, baked VIPM dependencies, and LabVIEW paths all target that single year, so choosing a different one asked the Windows worker jobs to pull a container image tag that was never published and made them fail. Locking the field removes that pitfall, and reinstalling or reconfiguring a repository that was previously pinned to an unsupported year now heals it back to the supported year. A future update will reintroduce this control as a code-compatibility selector that keeps the 2026 editor but saves your code compatible with an older LabVIEW run-time using the new project save setting.
highlights: The LabVIEW version selector is now read-only and fixed to the supported year (2026) in both the installer and Configure Workers.
This prevents the Windows container jobs from failing on a worker image tag that was never built for an unsupported year.
Reinstalling or reconfiguring a repository pinned to an old year now heals it back to the supported year.

releases.title: VI Browser 2.0: reliable render-token connect
releases.summary: Connecting a render token from the VI Browser 2.0 lock screen could appear to hang at the sealing-and-saving step and never finish. The underlying cause - a response read before it had arrived, which silently aborted the connect - is fixed, and the flow now reports each step (reading the repository key, loading the encryption library, saving the token, checking the render image) and times out with a clear, retryable message instead of hanging. The in-browser encryption library also falls back to a second, integrity-pinned CDN if the first is blocked. On success the lock dialog is replaced by a Connected confirmation that names the render image and offers a one-click button to run the snapshot action that populates VI Browser 2.0 with imagery, and the two-step token prompt now confirms the render token is captured while it collects the one-time setup token.
highlights: The render-token connect no longer hangs: each step reports progress and fails with a clear, retryable message.
The in-browser encryption library now has an integrity-pinned fallback CDN if the primary one is blocked.
On success the lock screen is replaced by a Connected panel with a one-click Generate the 2.0 snapshot button.

releases.title: Clients directory moves into the More menu
releases.summary: The Clients directory link now lives in the header's More (...) menu, alongside About and What's new, instead of as a primary navigation tab - a better fit for a management view. It still appears only on the source repository that originates the tooling, never on a consumer site, and opens the same page (with the Scan for clients button) that lists every public repository discovered to be running this tooling.
highlights: The Clients link is now in the header's More menu, next to About and What's new.
It still shows only on the root/source repository and never on a consumer site.
Same Clients page: lists discovered public repos and offers a Scan for clients button.

releases.title: Container packages: the tooling can now ship baked-in VIPM tools additively
releases.summary: Until now the worker image's VIPM package list (ci-tooling.packages.json) was entirely repo-owned and preserved across updates, so LabVIEW CI could never add a baked-in package to repositories that already installed it. A new tooling-managed file, ci-tooling.defaults.json, closes that gap: it is replaced by updates (it is not in userConfig.files), so packages added to it are pushed to every repo, while build-tooling-vipc.py merges them UNDER your own ci-tooling.packages.json. Your packages always win on a name collision - a pin you set is never overridden, and a differing default version is surfaced as a notice - and a package that exists only in the defaults is added to your worker the next time you regenerate the VIPC (Reconfigure or Update). Because every change still bumps this version, you see exactly when a new tool is offered and choose to adopt it. The defaults file starts empty, so existing workers rebuild byte-identically until a default is actually added.
highlights: New tooling-owned, version-locked ci-tooling.defaults.json lets LabVIEW CI ship baked-in VIPM packages to every repo, merged additively under your own list.
Your repo's packages are never clobbered: ci-tooling.packages.json stays preserved and wins on any name collision, with a notice when a default pins a different version.
No behavior change until a default is added - the defaults file starts empty and the generated worker VIPC is byte-identical.

releases.title: Antidoc: reliably find g-cli in the worker image
releases.summary: Antidoc documentation generation could fail with "g-cli was not found in the image" even when g-cli was baked into the worker. The g-cli installer adds itself to the machine PATH in the registry, but a Windows container does not inherit that change into its runtime PATH, so the runner could not see it. The Antidoc runner now refreshes its PATH from the registry (the equivalent of restarting a build agent after install) and falls back to the known install locations and a bounded search, so a worker that has Antidoc enabled finds g-cli and produces documentation.
highlights: The Antidoc runner refreshes PATH from the registry so a g-cli baked into the worker image is found at run time.
Added more g-cli install-location fallbacks plus a bounded search, covering the "G CLI" and "G-CLI" folder names.
Clearer diagnostics when g-cli genuinely is not present, pointing at enabling "Use Antidoc" and rebuilding the image.

releases.title: Clients directory: available on the source dashboard right away, with one-click rescan
releases.summary: The Clients directory now appears on the source repository's dashboard immediately - it no longer waits for the first scan to publish a list - and the page gained a Scan for clients button at the top that opens the discovery workflow so the list can be refreshed on demand. The not-yet-scanned and empty states now point you at that button. The entry still shows only on the root repository that originates the tooling and never on a consumer site.
highlights: The Clients menu shows on the source dashboard right away, before any scan has run.
A Scan for clients button on the page opens the discovery workflow to refresh the list on demand.
Not-yet-scanned and empty states now guide you to run a scan.

releases.title: VI Browser 2.0 wizard: enter the setup token without leaving the page
releases.summary: The VI Browser 2.0 setup wizard could dead-end: if this browser had not already stored a setup token (one with Secrets: write), pasting the render token and clicking Connect only told you to go set one up on the Integrate page. Connect - and the Generate step - now prompt for the missing token right on the lock screen, with a pre-scoped 'Create one' link; you paste it, it is kept only in your browser, and the action retries automatically. If your browser already has the token from installing, nothing changes.
highlights: Connect and Generate now request a missing setup or dispatch token inline, with a correctly-scoped create link, instead of redirecting to the Integrate page.
The render token stays a least-privilege read:packages token; the setup token is stored only in your browser and reused, so it is a one-time step.

releases.title: See who is running your tooling: a Clients directory on the dashboard
releases.summary: The source repository now publishes a Clients directory - a new top-level menu entry that lists every public repository discovered to be running this LabVIEW CI tooling and links straight to each one's dashboard. Discovery is pull-based: a scheduled workflow queries GitHub's code-search index for repositories that reference this tooling, verifies each is public and really installed it, and publishes the list to the Pages site, so nothing is asked of client repositories and no external service is involved. The Clients entry appears only on the source repository, where the list is published, and never on a consumer site. Apply-to-new-repo affordances now always source the tooling from this root repository, so every fresh install is discoverable and the count stays accurate.
highlights: A new source-only Clients menu lists every public repository running this tooling and links to each one's CI dashboard.
Discovery is pull-based via GitHub code search - clients are never modified and no external service or shared secret is needed; only public repositories are listed.
Every Apply-to-new-repo install now sources from the root repository, so new installs are discoverable and the client count stays accurate.

releases.title: VI Browser 2.0: set up the render token right on the lock screen
releases.summary: When a repository has not been provisioned for the in-place VI Browser 2.0 view, the lock screen is now an interactive setup wizard instead of two links. Step 1 opens GitHub's token page with the read:packages scope preselected; step 2 is an in-place box where you paste the render token, which is sealed in your browser (libsodium) and saved as the TOIMAGES_REGISTRY_TOKEN secret along with the render image variable, reusing the setup token already stored in this browser; and once that succeeds a 'Generate the 2.0 snapshot' button appears that dispatches the render workflow. The repository is detected from the page URL. Because a browser cannot test a private-registry pull, the dispatched run is the real confirmation that the token works.
highlights: The 2.0 lock screen is now a guided 3-step wizard: get a token, paste it to save the secret, then generate the snapshot - without leaving the page.
The render token is sealed in your browser and stored as the TOIMAGES_REGISTRY_TOKEN secret; the raw token never reaches the page host.
A 'Generate the 2.0 snapshot' button dispatches the render workflow and surfaces a token or permission error if the run cannot be queued.

releases.title: VI Browser 2.0: the 'Get a render token' link now points to the right repository
releases.summary: On the VI Browser 2.0 lock screen - shown when a repository has not yet been provisioned with a render token - the 'Get a render token' link pointed at the wrong URL, so it could not take you to the private render repository. The link now points to the correct repository, so anyone with access lands where they can obtain a token. Nothing else changed: the token is still entered on the Integrate page, sealed in your browser, and stored as the TOIMAGES_REGISTRY_TOKEN secret, and a repository without a token still shows the 1.0 view.
highlights: Corrected the 'Get a render token' link on the VI Browser 2.0 lock screen so it opens the right private render repository.
No change to entering or using the token - only the link target was fixed.

releases.title: The VI Browser opens with your folder tree expanded the first time
releases.summary: The first time you open the VI Browser, its file tree on the left now starts fully expanded, so the whole VI hierarchy is visible at a glance instead of a single collapsed folder. It is a one-time courtesy remembered in your browser: after that first visit the tree opens collapsed as before, and your own expand and collapse choices are never overridden. The Expand all and Collapse all controls behave exactly as before.
highlights: First-ever open of the VI Browser expands the entire folder tree, so the full VI hierarchy is visible without drilling in.
Remembered per browser as a one-time courtesy: later visits open collapsed, keeping the view tidy once you know your way around.
Your manual expand and collapse choices, and the Expand all / Collapse all buttons, are unchanged.

releases.title: Queued runs stay visible: still-queued, failed, or done - they no longer vanish
releases.summary: When you retroactively run activities for a fresh repository's whole history, GitHub only runs a limited number at once, so the rest sit queued - sometimes for many minutes. Until now the dashboard's optimistic 'Queued' badge timed out after 20 minutes and quietly reverted the cell to its run button, so runs that were still queued looked like they had vanished with no result. The dashboard now reconciles each queued badge against the real run status on GitHub: a run still queued or running keeps its 'Queued' badge for as long as it takes, a run that failed turns into a red 'Failed' badge that links to the run (an early failure may never post a status the dashboard could otherwise show), and a finished run hands off to the normal green/red result. Snapshot backfill runs - which share one history-walking run - are tracked too, and the badge's manage dialog gained a 'Dismiss' action so you can clear a failed cell and run it again.
highlights: Queued badges no longer disappear: a run still queued or running keeps its badge until it actually finishes, even past the old 20-minute timeout.
A run that fails now shows a red 'Failed' badge linking to the run, instead of silently reverting - so every cell reads as queued, failed, or done.
The History backfill's shared VI Snapshots run is tracked, and the manage dialog can Dismiss a failed cell so you can run it again.

releases.title: Update fix: a brand-new workflow file no longer breaks the no-token update path
releases.summary: Clicking Update now on a repository without a workflow-scope token is meant to defer just the .github/workflows/** files and still push the rest - the version bump, vendored scripts, and pages - so the new tooling adopts on its own and only the workflow YAMLs wait for an optional token. A gap remained: when a release introduced a brand-new workflow file (as v3.0.0 did with the Windows unit-test workflow), the deferral reverted changed workflow files but left newly-added ones staged, so the follow-up push was rejected again and the entire update failed - the version never moved. The deferral now strips every workflow change - modified, deleted, AND newly added - so config + scripts land and the version adopts; the workflow YAMLs are listed in the pull request and applied later once a TOOLING_UPDATE_TOKEN (repo + workflow scope) is added.
highlights: The no-token update path now also defers brand-new workflow files, not just modified ones, so the push is no longer rejected and the rest of the update lands.
Repositories without a TOOLING_UPDATE_TOKEN still adopt the new version (ref bump, scripts, pages); only the .github/workflows/** YAMLs wait for an optional repo + workflow scoped token.
No change for repositories that already have a workflow-scope token - those keep pushing the full update in a single commit.

releases.title: Unit testing, end to end: configure frameworks, run in CI, triage failures
releases.summary: A major release: unit testing is now a first-class part of the LabVIEW CI pipeline. On the new Unit Testing page you turn on the frameworks you use (JKI Caraya and JKI VI Tester) and say where each one's tests live - a folder or a glob. They run in the Windows worker, and their different outputs are merged into one Unit Tests report per revision on the dashboard, where you can run them on demand just like Mass Compile or VI Analyzer and click any failure to open the VI under test in the VI Browser. NI's Unit Test Framework is scaffolded for when its headless runner lands.
highlights: New Unit Testing configuration page: enable one or many frameworks and point each at its tests (directory or glob). Designed to scale to future tools.
Unit Tests is now in the activity matrix and the dashboard's Unit Tests column - run it on demand with the same Run-now flow as the other activities.
Every framework's results are normalised into one report; failing tests deep-link to the VI under test in the VI Browser.
Windows-only for now (Caraya and VI Tester are VIPM packages). The Caraya runner is the reference; execution is being validated on a real worker.

releases.title: Unit test results on the dashboard, plus pick your columns
releases.summary: The dashboard now has a Unit Tests column that shows pass/fail for your LabVIEW unit-test framework (NI Unit Test Framework, JKI VI Tester, or Caraya) once results are reported. A new Columns menu lets you choose which columns to see and remembers your choice, so the table stays readable as it grows. The per-worker 'Win Worker' and 'Linux Worker' columns were removed - which container ran an activity is reported with that activity (in its status and report), not as its own column.
highlights: New Unit Tests column reports pass/fail from your unit-test framework; which framework ran (UTF, JKI VI Tester, or Caraya) is recorded in the test report rather than as separate columns.
A Columns menu - a standard checklist - shows or hides individual columns and remembers your selection per repository.
Removed the Win Worker and Linux Worker columns to declutter the table; the worker image used stays available from each activity's status and report.
Commit stays pinned as the row identifier; every other column is optional.

releases.title: The backfill card explains how your runs are paced
releases.summary: The 'Populate the dashboard with your history' card now tells you how a big run will be paced before you start it. It shows the number of CI jobs this repository is set to run at once (its Max concurrent runners setting) and explains, in plain language, that anything past that - or past your GitHub account's own shared concurrency limit - simply waits its turn and runs in order, so it is safe to queue your whole history at once. A new 'How concurrency works' note clarifies that GitHub's real limit is per account (shared across every repository you own), not per repo, and includes a link that opens Configure Workers where you can change the limit for this repository.
highlights: The backfill card now shows how many CI jobs this repository runs at once - its Max concurrent runners setting - read from your configuration.
Plain-language reassurance that extra runs are queued and start automatically as earlier ones finish, so queuing all of history at once is safe.
A 'How concurrency works' disclosure explains that GitHub's limit is per account (shared across all your repositories), not per repo.
A link opens Configure Workers so you can change the concurrency setting for this repository.

releases.title: Populate history: choose a range or pick specific revisions
releases.summary: The Populate history dialog now lets you target exactly the revisions you want, not just everything from a starting point. A new 'Which revisions' control offers three scopes: All revisions (the full history), A range of history - work through revisions from a chosen start commit and, optionally, stop at a chosen commit - or Specific revision(s), a checklist where you tick exactly the one or several commits to run (with Select all / Clear shortcuts). Reversed range pickers are handled gracefully, the live summary updates as you choose, and everything still runs oldest to newest, queuing only the activities that have not run yet.
highlights: New 'Which revisions' scope control in Populate history: All revisions, a Range (start at a commit and optionally stop at one), or Specific revision(s).
Specific mode is a checklist of your revisions with Select all / Clear, so you can re-run just one commit or any handful you choose.
Range mode works through history from your chosen start to an optional stop point; a reversed start/stop is corrected automatically.
The live summary reflects the chosen scope, and runs still go oldest -> newest queuing only activities that have not run yet.

releases.title: VI Browser 2.0: navigate Case and Event structures in place
releases.summary: The VI Browser gains a 1.0 / 2.0 viewer switch, styled like the Windows/Linux tabs on a diff. 1.0 is the classic gallery you already know and stays the default, so nothing changes unless you opt in. 2.0 is a new in-place renderer that draws Case, Event, and Stacked Sequence structures right on the block diagram the way the LabVIEW editor shows them: each structure carries an on-diagram selector so you can page through its cases (including nested structures) without the diagram jumping, and the whole diagram pans and zooms. Your choice is remembered in the page URL, and 2.0 falls back to the 1.0 view (with a short note) for any VI that does not yet have the position data it needs. The snapshot pipeline is set up to produce that position data, so VIs start rendering in place in 2.0 automatically as it becomes available - existing snapshots and the classic view are unaffected.
highlights: New 'Viewer' 1.0 / 2.0 switch in the VI Browser toolbar (styled like the Windows/Linux diff tabs); 1.0, the classic flat gallery, remains the default so existing behavior is unchanged.

2.0 renders Case / Event / Stacked Sequence structures in place on the diagram - page through a structure's cases with an on-diagram selector, including nested structures - the way the LabVIEW editor shows them, with pan and zoom.
Your 1.0 / 2.0 choice persists in the page URL, and 2.0 gracefully falls back to the classic 1.0 view (with a note) for any VI that does not yet have position data.
The snapshot pipeline is prepared to emit the position-aware data that powers 2.0, so VIs light up in place automatically as it is generated; classic snapshots are untouched.

releases.title: Populate the dashboard with your project's history in a few clicks
releases.summary: A new Populate history action (in the dashboard header's menu) queues CI for the revisions that already exist in your repository, so a dashboard you just set up fills in with results instead of starting empty. You choose where in history to begin, whether to run a lean diff-based pass (VI Snapshots and VIDiff only - the visual history of what each revision changed) or every activity, and exactly which activities to queue. Everything runs oldest to newest so snapshots and diffs build on one another with no duplicated work, only activities that have not run yet are queued, and VI Snapshots backfills the whole history in a single incremental pass. The same choices are now offered from the fresh-install card via a new 'Choose activities or where to start' link, and a stored-cross-site-scripting issue where a crafted commit message could inject markup into the dashboard table has been fixed.
highlights: New 'Populate history' item in the dashboard header menu opens a dialog that queues CI for your existing revisions, so a freshly set-up dashboard fills in with results.

Pick where in history to start, choose a lean diff-based pass (VI Snapshots + VIDiff) or run every activity, and select exactly which activities to queue - with a live summary of how many runs will be queued.
Runs oldest -> newest with no duplicated work: only activities that have not run yet are queued, VIDiff skips root commits, and VI Snapshots backfills the whole history in one incremental pass.
The fresh-install card now links to the same dialog ('Choose activities or where to start'), and a stored-XSS issue with crafted commit messages in the dashboard table is fixed.

releases.title: A cleaner header: version and updates now live in one menu
releases.summary: The dashboard header has been tidied for a more professional, less cluttered look. The standalone version badge that used to sit in the top bar is gone; the installed version and the "What's new / Update" action now live together as a single entry inside the header's overflow (the three-dot More menu on desktop, the menu on mobile), so the version number no longer duplicates the update action. When an update is available - or one you started is still in progress - a single amber dot appears on the More button (or the hamburger on mobile), giving an at-a-glance cue without crowding the bar. A live "N running" chip now appears in the bar only while CI runs are in flight and disappears when they finish, so the bar stays clean when idle while still surfacing activity. This release also fixes a problem where an "Updating to v..." state begun on one repository's dashboard could briefly show on another repository's dashboard.
highlights: The always-on version badge is gone; the installed version and a "What's new" / "Update available" action are now a single entry in the header's More menu (and the mobile menu), removing the duplicate update surface.
A single amber dot on the More button (or the hamburger on mobile) signals at a glance when an update is available or in progress.
A live "N running" chip appears in the bar only while CI is active, then disappears - the bar stays uncluttered when idle while CI activity stays visible.
Fixes a cross-repository issue where an "Updating to v..." state started on one dashboard could appear on another; the in-progress flag is now scoped to its own repository.

releases.title: Configure when each activity runs, and a tidier Pipeline tab
releases.summary: The Configure dialog now spells out that its settings control what your CI does whenever a revision is submitted to the repository - a direct push to the default branch, a pull request from a branch in the repo, or a pull request from a fork. A new 'When activities run' table on the Pipeline tab lets you record, per activity, which of those sources should run it, with defaults that mirror how the pipeline already behaves (Mass Compile, VI Analyzer and VIDiff on pushes and pull requests; VI Snapshots only when a commit lands on the default branch). Fork pull requests are clearly flagged as running sandboxed by GitHub - read-only, with no access to your secrets. The long 'Max concurrent runners' explanation has also been tucked behind a click-to-open info (i) button so the form stays uncluttered.
highlights: New 'When activities run' table on the Pipeline tab: choose which submission sources - push to the default branch, pull request from a branch, or pull request from a fork - run each activity.
The dialog now states up front that these settings govern what happens whenever a revision is submitted to the repository.
Per-activity defaults match the current pipeline, so nothing changes until you edit them; your choices are saved to the configuration and travel with the reconfigure pull request.
Fork pull requests are flagged as sandboxed (read-only, no secrets), and the verbose concurrency explanation now lives behind a compact info button.

releases.title: The upgrade page shows the live upgrade instead of letting you restart it
releases.summary: When an update is already running, the What's New page now reflects that it is in progress and locks its controls, so you cannot accidentally start a second update. It shows live progress polled from GitHub - the workflow run, then the pull request, then the merge - and you can expand a panel to see the exact workflow jobs and steps as they execute. The page detects an in-flight update even if it was started from another device or browser, so clicking the dashboard's Updating badge always lands on an honest status instead of a button that offers to start the update again.
highlights: The upgrade page now recognises an update already in progress - even one started elsewhere - by checking GitHub directly, not just this browser.
While an update is in flight the controls lock, so a duplicate update can never be started; the page points you to approving and merging the open pull request.
Expand the what-is-running panel to see the exact workflow jobs and steps of the update as they execute.
Clear, honest status replaces the old behaviour where the in-progress badge led to a page that offered to restart the update.

releases.title: Choose which VIPC files to bake into the worker container
releases.summary: The Configure dialog's Worker container section now lists every VIPC file found in your repository and lets you choose which ones to bake into the worker image. Discovered files are de-selected by default, so nothing new is added to the container until you tick it — you pick exactly the dependency files the project needs. Files already configured in your pipeline stay selected and keep their Monitor setting, so existing containers are unaffected. Once a file is included you can still turn on Monitor to rebuild and re-push the container automatically whenever that file changes.
highlights: The Worker container section pre-populates with every .vipc file discovered in the repository, so you no longer have to type each path by hand.
Each discovered file has an include checkbox and is de-selected by default — only the files you tick are baked into the worker image.
Files already baked in your existing configuration load pre-selected and keep their Monitor setting, so current pipelines are unchanged.
Turn on Monitor for an included file to rebuild and re-push the container automatically whenever that file changes.

releases.title: Backfill CI for your whole history in one click
releases.summary: A brand-new dashboard starts empty because nothing has run yet. Now, on a fresh install, the dashboard shows a card at the top that offers to queue CI for every existing revision in a single click. It processes history oldest commit first and works forward, so snapshots and diffs build on one another instead of being recomputed: VI snapshots run as one backfill pass that walks the whole history and reuses what is already rendered, and each VIDiff is queued once against its real parent. Mass Compile, VI Analyzer, VIDiff and snapshots are all covered. The card disappears once anything is queued and can be dismissed (remembered per repository). It reuses the same token you set the dashboard up with, so there is no second token to create. This release also fixes dispatching runs on repositories whose default branch is not 'main' (for example 'master'), which previously failed with an HTTP 422 error.
highlights: New 'Run all history' card on a fresh dashboard queues CI for every revision in one click, oldest first.
Snapshots run as a single oldest-to-newest backfill that seeds from what is already deployed, so nothing is rendered twice; diffs are queued once each against their true parent.
The card auto-hides once anything has been queued, and can be dismissed (remembered per repository).
Reuses the token you set the dashboard up with (Apply to New Repo) to queue runs, so no second token is needed; that setup token now also requests Actions: Read and write.
Fixed: queuing runs now works on repositories whose default branch is not 'main' (e.g. 'master'), which previously returned HTTP 422.

releases.title: Safer default for concurrent runners, with clearer GitHub limits guidance
releases.summary: The Configure dialog's Max concurrent runners now defaults to 5 instead of 20, and explains an easy-to-miss detail about GitHub Actions: the simultaneous-jobs limit is per account, not per repository. GitHub's standard-runner concurrency cap (20 on Free, 40 on Pro, 60 on Team, 500 on Enterprise) is shared across every repository you own, and routine work such as submitting code or running a tooling upgrade draws from that same pool. So a high per-repo number, multiplied across several repositories, can quietly oversubscribe your account and leave jobs queued. The new conservative default leaves headroom, and the dialog, the reconfigure workflow, and the FAQ now spell out the per-account vs per-repo distinction so you can choose a number deliberately. The per-repo setting records your preferred ceiling, while GitHub's account-wide limit is what actually schedules and queues jobs.
highlights: Max concurrent runners now defaults to 5 (was 20), leaving headroom under GitHub's per-account concurrency limit.
Clear guidance everywhere it matters: GitHub's simultaneous-jobs limit (Free 20, Pro 40, Team 60, Enterprise 500) is per account and shared across all your repositories, not per repo.
Reminds you that ordinary submissions and tooling upgrades also consume from that shared pool, so spreading CI across many repos adds up; pick a per-repo number judiciously.
FAQ corrected: included GitHub Actions minutes are 2,000/month on Free and 3,000/month on Pro and Team.

releases.title: Live progress and locked controls while CI tooling installs or updates
releases.summary: The dashboard's Update now and the Apply to New Repo configurator now show clear, live progress so you can always tell work is happening — and they lock their controls while that work runs so you can't accidentally start the same job twice. Update now follows the real server-side update as it happens: a five-step tracker (Start, Build update, Open PR, Approve checks, Merge) advances as the update workflow is queued, runs, and opens its pull request, then highlights that it's your turn to approve and merge, and turns green once the PR is merged. While those automatic steps run, the version picker and buttons are disabled; they re-enable the moment the pull request is open. If you reload mid-update, the page restores the live tracker and keeps following along. Apply to New Repo gains a progress bar that fills through each real phase of the install (reading the repo, fetching files, committing, opening the pull request) and disables the whole form while installing, so your choices can't change underneath you; to set up another repository, just change the repo and install again.
highlights: Update now shows a live five-step tracker that follows the real update workflow run and the pull request it opens, then clearly flags when it's your turn to approve and merge.
All update controls lock while the automatic steps run — no more accidentally dispatching the same update twice — and unlock as soon as the pull request is open.
Reloading during an update restores the live progress and keeps tracking it, instead of looking idle.
Apply to New Repo now has a progress bar through each install phase and disables the form while installing; to apply to another repository, change the repo and install again.

releases.title: The dashboard tells you when it's being rebuilt
releases.summary: While the workflow that regenerates the dashboard (or the GitHub Pages publish that follows it) is running, the dashboard now shows a banner at the top: it says a new version is being compiled, links directly to the running action, and refreshes the page automatically once that action completes. No more staring at a stale dashboard wondering whether your latest change has landed yet.
highlights: A banner at the top of the dashboard appears whenever the page is being rebuilt, with a live link to the GitHub Actions run doing the work.
The page reloads itself once the rebuild finishes, so the new version shows up without a manual refresh.
It appears only on the dashboard, and only while a rebuild or Pages publish is actually in flight.

releases.title: Organize Configure into tabs and pick a container per action
releases.summary: The Configure dialog is reorganized into clear tabs — Pipeline, Container & Dependencies, Action Routing, and Review & Apply — so each concern is easy to find instead of one long page. The new Action Routing tab lets you choose which container each Windows action runs in: Mass Compile, VI Analyzer, VIDiff, and VI Snapshots can each use the project default, the latest build, a pinned build, the bare NI base image, or a custom tag, saved to config.container.actions and read by the matching workflow at run time. The Container tab gathers the default worker container, the VIPC dependency files baked into it (with per-file Monitor to rebuild and re-push to GHCR automatically when a file changes), and a new Build / update the container now action that dispatches the image build on demand. Existing pipelines are unaffected — with nothing set, every action keeps using the same image it did before.
highlights: Configure is now tabbed (Pipeline, Container & Dependencies, Action Routing, Review & Apply) so settings are far easier to navigate.

Action Routing: choose the container per action (Latest, a pinned build, the bare NI base image, or a custom tag) — e.g. pin VIDiff to a frozen build while Mass Compile tracks Latest. Stored in config.container.actions; Mass Compile, VI Analyzer, and VIDiff read it on Windows.
Specify one or more VIPC files to bake into the worker image, and turn on Monitor so a change to that file automatically rebuilds and re-pushes the container to your GHCR.
New Build / update the container now action creates a new image version or updates the existing one in GHCR on demand, without waiting for a file change.
Backward compatible: with no per-action overrides, every action uses config.container.use exactly as before.

releases.title: Fix (again) the blank dashboard on master / non-main repos
releases.summary: A stale-file commit had silently reverted the dashboard generator's default-branch detection, so releases v2.1.1–v2.1.4 shipped a generator that always asked GitHub for commits on a 'main' branch. Any repository whose default branch is 'master' (or anything other than 'main') therefore built a completely empty dashboard — it fetched zero commits even though the history was right there. This restores the fix: the generator now queries the repository's real default branch (cached) and fetches commits from it, and the bare repo API call no longer sends a trailing slash that GitHub answers with 404. Verified against a 'master'-default repo: 22 commits, 20 project revisions.
highlights: The dashboard again detects the repo's real default branch instead of assuming 'main', so 'master' (and other) default branches show their commits.
Fixes the regression behind a dashboard that stayed blank with 'No project revisions' despite a full commit history.
Hardened the API helper + added a guard comment so the empty-path call can't silently regress to a 404-causing trailing slash again.

releases.title: Update now fixes the dashboard without a token
releases.summary: Clicking Update now used to push every refreshed file — including GitHub workflow YAMLs — onto one branch. Repositories without a workflow-scope token (no TOOLING_UPDATE_TOKEN secret) had that push rejected wholesale, so nothing landed: the version never bumped and a dashboard pinned to an older, buggy tooling ref stayed blank. Now the update is resilient. When the only thing blocking the push is workflow scope, it defers just the .github/workflows/** files and pushes the rest — the labview-ci.yml ref bump, vendored scripts, and pages — with the built-in token. Because the dashboard and reusable workflows load the tooling at that ref at runtime, the version (and the dashboard fix) adopts on its own; the deferred workflow YAMLs are listed in the PR and applied later once an optional token is added.
highlights: Update now adopts the new version token-lessly: the config ref bump + scripts + pages always push with the built-in token, so the dashboard updates even on repos with no TOOLING_UPDATE_TOKEN.
Only changes to .github/workflows/** still need a one-time TOOLING_UPDATE_TOKEN (repo + workflow scope); they are deferred and clearly reported instead of failing the whole update.
Fixes the case where a repo's dashboard stayed blank after Update now because the ref bump never landed (the thin caller kept checking out the old, buggy tooling).

releases.title: Starting an update affects only the repository you started it from
releases.summary: Starting a tooling update from one repository's dashboard no longer shows an 'Updating...' badge on your other repositories. Because every dashboard is served from the same GitHub Pages site (a single browser origin), the in-progress update indicator could appear on all of your repositories at once even though only the one you clicked was actually updating. The update state is now scoped to the specific repository you started the update from, so your other dashboards stay clear and keep showing their own version.
highlights: Clicking the version badge or 'Update now' shows the 'Updating to v...' badge only on the repository you started the update from.
Other repositories' dashboards no longer display a false in-progress update just because they share the same Pages site and browser storage.
Only the in-progress indicator was scoped per repository; how updates are dispatched and applied is unchanged.

releases.title: Fix cascading dashboard builds on fresh installs
releases.summary: Removed the broad 'on: status' trigger from the dashboard workflow that was causing cascading rebuilds during fresh CI installs. When multiple CI workflows started simultaneously (Mass Compile, VI Analyzer, etc.), each status update would trigger a dashboard rebuild, creating a backlog of running builds. The dashboard continues to update correctly via workflow_run, push, schedule, and manual triggers—this change just eliminates the noise.
highlights: Fresh installs no longer spawn cascading dashboard builds from status events.
Dashboard still updates after CI workflows complete and on config changes.
Reduces GitHub Actions usage and clears the Actions tab of duplicate builds on initial setup.

releases.title: Dashboard works on all default branches (fixed trailing slash bug)
releases.summary: A bug in the dashboard generator prevented it from working on repositories with non-main default branches. The get_default_branch() function correctly detected the repo's default branch and requested commits from it, but the URL construction inadvertently added a trailing slash (https://api.github.com/repos/{repo}/ instead of https://api.github.com/repos/{repo}), causing GitHub API to return 404. This prevented the dashboard from populating with any commits on repos using master, develop, or other non-main branch names. Fixed by only adding '/' before the path parameter when path is non-empty.
highlights: The dashboard generator now correctly handles repositories with non-main default branches without returning empty dashboards.
Specifically fixes the case where repos with master, develop, or other default branches would show 'No project revisions in the recent window' even with abundant commit history.
Applied to both Extensible-Config-Dialog and any other repo using a non-main default branch.

releases.title: Manage your worker container and its VIPC dependencies from Configure
releases.summary: The Configure dialog can now manage the container your CI runs in. A new Worker container section lets you choose which prebuilt image future actions use — the latest build, so your project's dependencies always stay current, or a specific pinned build for reproducibility — and lists the VIPC dependency files baked into that image. Each VIPC file can be set to Monitor, so the container is rebuilt and pushed to GHCR automatically whenever that file changes; files you don't monitor are baked once and left pinned. The worker image build always publishes a :latest tag, bakes exactly the VIPC files you list (from anywhere in the repo), and skips a rebuild when the only change is a VIPC you chose not to monitor. VI Analyzer, Mass Compile, and VIDiff on Windows all run inside the container you select. Existing pipelines keep working unchanged — with nothing configured, every activity uses the same image it did before.
highlights: New “Worker container” section in Configure: pick which prebuilt GHCR image future actions use — Latest (auto-updates as your dependencies change) or a pinned build for reproducibility, with the current build offered as a one-click pin.

List the VIPC dependency files baked into the worker image and turn on Monitor per file, so the container is rebuilt and pushed to GHCR automatically whenever that file changes — keeping the container in sync with the dependencies the project needs.
The worker image build always publishes a :latest tag, bakes exactly the VIPC files you configure, and skips a rebuild when the only change is a VIPC you chose not to monitor.
VI Analyzer, Mass Compile, and VIDiff on Windows now run inside the container you select; with nothing configured they keep using their previous image, so existing pipelines are unaffected.

releases.title: Reports open inside the dashboard header instead of replacing it
releases.summary: Opening a Mass Compile or VI Analyzer report from the dashboard now keeps the shared navigation header in place: the report loads framed inside the dashboard chrome — same brand, navigation, version badge, a revision picker, and a Regenerate / Re-run button — rather than navigating away to a bare full-page report. This also means reports that predate the header (or carry no header of their own) still appear inside the chrome, so older Mass Compile and VI Analyzer reports gain the navigation and a one-click re-run without being regenerated. Switching revisions from the header re-frames the same viewer, and a report's own copy of the header is hidden while it is framed so there is always exactly one header.
highlights: The dashboard's Mass Compile and VI Analyzer links now open the report framed under the shared header (via report-viewer.html) instead of as a bare page, so the header no longer disappears when you open a report.

Reports that carry no header of their own — including ones published before the header existed — now appear inside the chrome too, with the navigation, version badge, and a Regenerate / Re-run button, without needing to be regenerated.
The header's revision picker re-frames the viewer onto the chosen revision (keeping the chrome), and the Raw log / Native report link follows the framed report.
A failed run whose status links to its Actions page (not a report) still opens that run, and the diff / snapshots links continue to open the VI Browser as before.

releases.title: The version badge shows when CI is running
releases.summary: The navigation header's version badge now doubles as a live activity indicator: whenever workflow runs are queued or in progress, it replaces the version number with a spinning 'N running' badge that links to the Actions tab, then returns to the version once everything finishes. This makes the dashboard visibly reflect work in progress — for example a deploy you're waiting on right after 'Apply to New Repo' or an update — instead of looking stale while it catches up. It reads the repository's in-flight runs from the browser, so public repositories need no setup and private repositories reuse the dashboard's existing token; it only checks while the tab is open and falls back to the plain version number if activity can't be read. An in-progress tooling update still takes precedence and shows 'Updating to v…'.
highlights: While any workflow run is queued or in progress, the version badge becomes a live 'N running' indicator with a spinner and links to the Actions tab; it reverts to the version number when the runs finish.
Appears on the dashboard and every page that carries the shared header, and refreshes as runs start and finish (only while the tab is visible).
Public repositories work with no setup; private repositories reuse the dashboard's existing dispatch token, and the badge quietly falls back to the version number when activity can't be read.
An in-progress tooling update ('Updating to v…') and the 'update available' cue still take priority over the running indicator.

releases.title: Apply to New Repo detects an existing install and lets you keep settings or clear out old files
releases.summary: Re-running 'Apply to New Repo' on a repository that already has LabVIEW CI now notices the previous install and pauses to ask how to reconcile it, before anything is committed. You can keep the previous capabilities, LabVIEW version, and platforms (instead of the configurator's current selections), and you can have old tooling files that newer versions no longer use — for example workflows that were renamed or dropped — removed in the same pull request. Both are opt-in and listed up front, your own workflows and baked dependency files are never touched, and everything still lands in a reviewable pull request.
highlights: Detects a prior install by reading the repo's .github/labview-ci.yml and prompts before committing — fresh repos are unaffected and install as before.
'Keep the existing settings' reuses the previously installed capabilities, LabVIEW version, and platforms instead of the configurator's current picks.

'Remove old tooling files no longer used' lists and deletes orphaned tooling files (e.g. renamed/removed workflows) in the install pull request; it recognizes tooling files by content, so your own workflows and baked VIPM dependency files are never removed.
The result summary and pull request spell out exactly what was kept and which files were removed.

releases.title: Every Mass Compile report opens inside the dashboard
releases.summary: Mass Compile reports now always render inside the shared navigation header — even the minimal fallback report. Previously, if the friendly report builder was skipped or hit a snag, the run still published a plain log-dump page with no header, navigation, or version badge, so a few Mass Compile links opened a bare page outside the dashboard chrome. The safety-net report (both Windows and Linux) now carries the same header as the full report, with the revision picker and the Regenerate / Raw log / This commit actions. Every previously published Mass Compile report has also been refreshed in place so it shows the header too.
highlights: The fallback Mass Compile report (emitted when the friendly builder is skipped) now includes the shared site header, so every Mass Compile report renders inside the dashboard chrome — brand, navigation, version badge, and the revision picker.
All previously published Mass Compile reports were refreshed to show the header as well; the figures are unchanged — only the chrome was added.

releases.title: The header shows which repository you're viewing
releases.summary: The navigation header's top-left mark now shows the repository name — with a small LabVIEW CI label above it — instead of just the product name, so the repository whose results you're viewing is always visible, on the dashboard and on every report and page that carries the header. The name truncates with an ellipsis when space is tight (the full owner/name shows on hover), so the header stays tidy and fits all the way down to a narrow phone.
highlights: The header's top-left brand now reads the repository name with a small LabVIEW CI kicker above it, so you can always tell which repository's dashboard or report you're looking at.
Responsive by design: the repository name truncates with an ellipsis (full owner/name on hover) and never collides with the mobile menu button, so the header fits cleanly on phones.

releases.title: The 'create a token' links arrive with the settings pre-filled
releases.summary: When you set up the one-time token for Run now or Update now, the 'Create a fine-grained token' link now opens GitHub's form with the token name, the resource owner, and the Actions: Read and write permission already filled in — so you only have to pick the repository under Repository access (the one step GitHub gives no way to pre-fill from a link) and click Generate. The report header's Re-run / Regenerate token link already worked this way; now the dashboard and What's New match it.
highlights: The Run now token setup (dashboard) and the Update now token setup (What's New) now pre-fill the token name, resource owner, and Actions: Read and write permission via GitHub's supported URL parameters.
Each link is clear that selecting the specific repository is the only manual step — GitHub provides no URL parameter to pre-select a repository.

releases.title: Your pipeline is pinned to a version, and every report shares one header
releases.summary: Two things. First, an install or upgrade now pins your whole pipeline — the dashboard, the workflows, the report pages, everything — to the exact version you adopted. It stays on that version until you click Update now; it no longer tracks the source repo's latest changes automatically. (Previously the dashboard was rebuilt from the source's main branch on every run, so dashboard/header changes appeared before you upgraded.) The update check still tells you when a newer version is available. Second, the shared navigation header is now on every document the pipeline publishes — Mass Compile reports and the worker/container manifest pages join the dashboard, VI Analyzer reports, and the VI Browser. On a VI Analyzer or Mass Compile report the header also carries a revision picker (switch which revision's report you're viewing, just like the VI Browser's commit dropdown) and a one-click “regenerate this report for this revision” button.
highlights: Installs and upgrades pin the consumer's source.ref to the exact version tag (v<version>), so the dashboard and all tooling only change when you run Update now — not whenever the source repo's main advances. The update-available check is unaffected (it reads the source's release channel).
The shared header now appears on Mass Compile reports and the worker/container manifest pages too, so navigation and branding are consistent across every published document.

VI Analyzer and Mass Compile reports gain a header revision picker — pick another revision to jump to that revision's report of the same type (the list only offers revisions that actually have that report).
Those reports also get a “Re-run analysis” / “Regenerate report” button in the header that dispatches a fresh run for the revision shown, reusing the dashboard's token and queued-run indicator.

releases.title: Header fits the VI Browser, and a commit hash opens its VIs everywhere
releases.summary: Fixes the new navigation header on full-height pages: on the VI Browser the header was being laid out beside the sidebar instead of across the top, because that page uses its <body> as a side-by-side layout. The header now detects a page that lays its content out directly on <body> and tucks that content beneath itself, so it spans the top on every page. Separately, the commit hash in the dashboard's Commit column now opens that commit's VIs in the VI Browser — the same place the commit message already linked to — instead of the commit on GitHub, so both columns take you to the snapshots for that revision.
highlights: The shared header now spans the full width at the top of the VI Browser (and any page that uses <body> as a flex/grid layout) instead of sitting beside the content.
On the dashboard, the Commit-column hash links to that commit's VIs in the VI Browser, matching the commit message link (it previously went to the commit on GitHub).

releases.title: Jump from a VI Analyzer report straight to that commit's VI snapshots
releases.summary: On a VI Analyzer report, the commit hash at the top now links to that exact revision's VI snapshots in the VI Browser, so you can go from a finding to seeing the VIs as they were at that commit in one click. The GitHub commit itself is still one click away — a 'commit on GitHub' link sits alongside the metadata (and the header's 'This commit' action still opens it on a standalone report).
highlights: The commit hash on a VI Analyzer report links to that commit's VI snapshots in the VI Browser (it previously pointed at GitHub, which the report header's 'This commit' action already covers).
A 'commit on GitHub' link is kept in the report's metadata line so the commit is still reachable even when the report is viewed embedded (where the shared header hides itself).

releases.title: A consistent navigation header across the whole CI site
releases.summary: Every page the pipeline publishes — the dashboard, VI Analyzer reports, the VI Browser, and the Configure / Apply to New Repo pages — now shares one navigation header instead of each page carrying its own ad-hoc buttons in a corner. The header gives you the LabVIEW CI brand, links to the main areas, and context-aware actions for the page you're on: the dashboard surfaces Configure Workers and Apply to New Repo, while a VI Analyzer report surfaces Re-run analysis, Native report, and This commit. It is fully responsive (the links and actions collapse into a single menu on phones and narrow windows) and is deployed once as a shared asset, so it stays identical everywhere and survives as individual reports are added or regenerated. The version badge and these What's New notes now live in the header too.
highlights: One shared header on every page — dashboard, VI Analyzer reports, VI Browser, Configure, Apply to New Repo, and What's New — so you can move between areas from anywhere with a consistent brand and navigation.

Context-aware actions live in the header: the dashboard offers Configure Workers and Apply to New Repo; a VI Analyzer report offers Re-run analysis (for that exact revision), Native report, and This commit.
Fully responsive — the navigation and actions collapse into a single menu button on phones and narrow windows, so the whole site is usable on mobile.

The header is a single asset deployed once at the site root, so it stays identical across pages, survives as reports come and go, and hides itself automatically when a report is embedded inside another page.

releases.title: One-click install is reliable for a full-capability repo (no more “Load failed”)
releases.summary: Installing the pipeline into a repo from Apply to New Repo could fail partway through with “Load failed”. Since 1.10.1 pre-selects every capability, the install uploaded ~60 files as a separate blob request each, in a tight burst that trips GitHub's secondary rate limit (whose CORS-less response surfaces in the browser as a bare “Load failed”). The install now commits all text files in a single tree request (so only a handful of binary VIs are uploaded individually) and retries transient network / rate-limit hiccups with backoff, with a clear message and command-line fallback if anything still fails. Re-running the install is now a dependable way to put a repo's CI tooling back into a known-good state.
highlights: Text files are committed in one git/trees request instead of one blob upload each — ~60 back-to-back API writes drop to ~13 — so a full-capability install no longer trips GitHub's secondary rate limit.

Every GitHub call retries network rejections and secondary-rate-limit / 5xx responses with backoff (honouring Retry-After); a persistent failure shows a clear “wait a minute and retry, or use the command line” message instead of “Load failed”.
Re-installing into a repo that already has the tooling reliably converges it to the current known-good state (the existing install branch / pull request is reused).

releases.title: Apply to New Repo selects every capability by default
releases.summary: The Apply to New Repo configurator now pre-selects all available capabilities (mass compile, VI Analyzer, VIDiff, VI snapshots/VI Browser, the shared container image, and the dashboard) instead of only the recommended ones, so a new install gets the full pipeline by default. Uncheck anything you don't want before installing; capabilities still marked Coming soon stay disabled.
highlights: All non-planned capabilities are checked by default in the configurator — the VI snapshots/VI Browser and shared container image capabilities are no longer off by default.
The capabilities hint now reads “All available capabilities are selected by default — uncheck any you don't want.”

releases.title: Re-run a VI Analyzer report for its revision — right from the report
releases.summary: A VI Analyzer report now has a “Re-run analysis for this revision” button that dispatches a fresh analyzer run for exactly that commit, without hunting for the workflow on GitHub. It reuses the same one-time fine-grained token the dashboard's Run now uses (stored only in your browser), and the moment the run is queued the matching cell on the dashboard shows a working/“Queued” spinner — even if that cell already had a pass/fail result — then settles back to the new result when the run finishes. The report and dashboard share that signal because both are served from the same Pages site.
highlights: New “↻ Re-run analysis for this revision” button in the VI Analyzer report dispatches run-vi-analyzer for that exact commit (same platform that produced the report), with an inline token prompt and clear 401/403/404 guidance.

The dashboard cell for that revision immediately shows a “Queued” spinner via the same optimistic overlay as Run now — now able to overlay a cell that already holds a result — and self-clears once a newer result lands or the run reports in.
Re-uses the dashboard's lvci_dispatch_token and queued-runs bridge (report and dashboard are same-origin on Pages), so no new token or setup is needed if you've already used Run now.

releases.title: Update now never leaves you with a conflicting pull request
releases.summary: Clicking Update now used to open a new per-version pull request each time, so unmerged ones piled up; once your default branch moved on, those stale branches collided on catalog.json and labview-ci.yml and showed a merge conflict — even though the update overwrites those files wholesale and there was nothing real to resolve. The update workflow now keeps exactly one open update pull request: when it opens or refreshes the newest one, it automatically closes every older update PR and deletes its branch. The pull request body also explains the self-heal: if an update PR ever shows a conflict, click Update now again and the branch is rebuilt from the latest default branch, which clears it.
highlights: The Apply update workflow auto-closes superseded update PRs (older ci/adopt-tooling-* branches) and deletes them, so only the newest update PR — always based on the current default branch — remains.
A merge conflict on an update PR is no longer something to hand-resolve: re-running Update now rebuilds the branch from the latest default branch and clears it.
The update PR body now spells this out, so a stale or conflicting update PR is self-explanatory instead of a dead end.

releases.title: The dashboard now publishes itself when you merge the install — no waiting, no manual trigger
releases.summary: After installing, the CI dashboard workflow had no trigger that fired on the install merge — only on commit statuses, after other CI workflows, or on its hourly schedule — so a brand-new install showed the old page (or nothing) until something else happened to run it. The generated dashboard workflow now also builds on a push to your default branch that touches its config, so merging the install pull request publishes the dashboard right away. The one-time install token is also now session-scoped and discarded as soon as the pull request opens.
highlights: The generated dashboard-pages.yml gains a push trigger on your default branch (scoped to .github/labview-ci.yml + the workflow itself), so merging the install PR builds and publishes the dashboard immediately instead of waiting for the hourly schedule.
The push trigger targets the repository's actual default branch (e.g. main or master), resolved at install time; applies to the one-click install, the downloadable bundle, and install.py.
The one-click install token is now kept in sessionStorage and automatically discarded the moment the install pull request opens — it never persists beyond the browser tab.

releases.title: VI Analyzer reports were empty - the CI worker now ships and runs the full analyzer test suite
releases.summary: Every VI Analyzer report came back empty (0 VIs, 0 tests, no findings). Root cause: the Windows CI worker image is built on a base where the VI Analyzer Toolkit is registered as installed but its default test libraries (~90 tests) had been stripped, so the analyzer loaded but ran zero tests. The worker image build now reinstalls the VI Analyzer support package when those test libraries are missing and fails the build if they are still absent, so a worker that cannot analyze anything can never be published. The analyzer configuration and runner were also fixed to select the full default test suite (an empty test list previously meant 'run nothing'), and the runner script was made ASCII-clean so Windows PowerShell parses it reliably.
highlights: Worker image now verifies the VI Analyzer default test suite is actually on disk after install - it reinstalls the support package if the tests are missing and fails the build if they still are, so a test-less worker is never published.
Analyzer configuration ships a populated test list (an empty test list made LabVIEW run zero tests); the runner auto-selects the full installed suite and falls back to a known-good set.
Runner script kept pure-ASCII so Windows PowerShell 5.1 parses it - a stray non-ASCII character had broken the analysis step.

releases.title: Installed dashboards now actually build, and the README link is added even when an old dashboard URL is present
releases.summary: Two install fixes. (1) The dashboard workflow a fresh install writes is now a thin caller that checks the tooling out at runtime and runs ./_lvci/actions/dashboard, instead of the source's own dashboard-pages.yml that referenced a local ./actions/dashboard path no consumer has — which made the dashboard build fail with 'Can't find action.yml under actions/dashboard'. (2) The 'add a dashboard link to the README' step now decides idempotency only by its own marker comment, so a repository that already mentions the Pages URL (for example a previous/precursor dashboard's badges) still gets the link added.
highlights: Fresh installs write a thin dashboard-pages.yml (checkout tooling into _lvci at the opted-in ref, then ./_lvci/actions/dashboard) so the generator is always the source's own and never needs vendoring or rebranding — fixes the failing dashboard build.
